data.tagline: Rewrite reality through psychic power that was never meant for mortal minds.
data.overview: Aberrant Mind shifts the sorcerer toward telepathy, mental manipulation, and subtle spellcasting. It excels at controlling minds, gathering information, and casting powerful magic without obvious signs, making it dangerous both inside and outside combat. Mechanically, it offers exceptional flexibility and efficiency, giving the sorcerer more tools than most origins while maintaining its devastating magical offense.

data.tagline: Breezy and untethered.
data.overview:
Air genasi are descended from djinn, the genies of the Elemental Plane of Air. Embodying many of the airy traits of their otherworldly ancestors, air genasi can draw upon their connection to the winds.

Air genasi’s skin tones include many shades of blue, along with the full range of human skin tones, with bluish or ashen casts. Sometimes their skin is marked by lines that seem like cracks with bluish-white energy spilling out. An air genasi’s hair might blow in a phantom wind or be made entirely of clouds or vapor.

definingFeature.name: Unending Breath
definingFeature.desc: Hold your breath indefinitely; lightning resistance and Levitate at higher level.

data.tagline: Every problem has a formula—it's just a matter of mixing the right ingredients.
data.overview: The Alchemist specializes in experimental elixirs, restorative magic, and enhancing allies through magical concoctions. Rather than overwhelming enemies with direct damage, it provides healing, buffs, and flexible support while supplementing its spells with chemical creations. Playing an Alchemist feels like constantly looking for the right solution to the current problem, making it one of the most support-oriented artificer paths.

The Arcana Domain is the cleric for players who want to stand at the intersection of divine power and arcane magic. While most clerics focus primarily on healing, protection, or direct intervention from their deity, Arcana clerics expand their toolkit by borrowing from the wizard's world of spells and magical knowledge. They gain access to arcane cantrips and abilities that make them far more versatile problem-solvers than many other clerics, allowing them to contribute in situations that normally fall outside the class's traditional strengths. An Arcana cleric still serves as a capable healer and support character, but their deeper understanding of magic itself makes them particularly effective against magical threats, enemy spellcasters, and supernatural phenomena. If a standard cleric channels divine power to shape the world, an Arcana cleric seeks to understand the underlying rules that govern magic and use that knowledge to their advantage.
data.tagline: A divine scholar who blends the miracles of a cleric with the versatility and magical expertise of a wizard.

data.overview: Arcane Archers transform ordinary shots into precise magical attacks capable of hindering, damaging, or manipulating enemies. Rather than casting spells directly, they enhance key attacks with specialized arcane techniques that reward accuracy and careful timing. Playing one feels deliberate, choosing exactly the right enchanted arrow to change the course of a battle.

data.tagline: A magician who lies, cheats, and steals with illusion and enchantment.
data.overview: Arcane Tricksters blend the Rogue's agility with a limited selection of magical spells, using illusions, enchantments, and magical tricks to deceive enemies and overcome obstacles. Their magic enhances their existing toolkit rather than replacing it, opening countless creative solutions both inside and outside combat. This subclass rewards players who enjoy improvisation and solving problems in unconventional ways.

data.tagline: Become the weapon by turning enchanted armor into an extension of yourself.
data.overview: The Armorer transforms magical armor into its defining invention, allowing the artificer to serve as either a durable frontline defender or a stealthy ranged combatant. Its armor evolves alongside the character, providing defensive strength, mobility, and specialized weapon systems without relying on conventional equipment. The subclass feels like a customizable powered suit, encouraging players to adapt their role to the needs of the party.

data.tagline: An inventor who infuses items with magic.
data.overview: Artificers are inventors, engineers, and magical craftsmen who approach spellcasting as a science rather than a mystical gift. Instead of simply casting spells, they create magical devices, infuse ordinary equipment with lasting enchantments, and solve problems through preparation and ingenuity. Their strength lies in versatility—an artificer can adapt to nearly any situation by combining magic, technology, and clever planning. Whether supporting allies, enhancing equipment, or bringing specialized inventions into battle, they excel at making both themselves and their team more capable.

definingFeature.name: Infuse Item
definingFeature.desc: Imbue mundane gear with magical infusions, equipping yourself and allies with custom magic items.

lore.role:
Flex / Support / Force Multiplier

Artificers specialize in making themselves and their allies better at what they already do. Through infused equipment, magical inventions, and carefully chosen spells, they can reinforce weaknesses, amplify strengths, or temporarily reshape the party's capabilities to meet the challenges ahead. An artificer may spend one day turning a frontline fighter into an unstoppable defensive wall, the next day enhancing a damage dealer's effectiveness, or adjusting their own equipment to fill a role the group is missing. While they can contribute respectable damage, control, or defense themselves, their greatest strength is often increasing the effectiveness of everyone around them.
lore.playstyle:
Artificer rewards players who enjoy understanding how a party functions as a system. Much of the class's power comes from recognizing which allies benefit most from additional armor, mobility, accuracy, utility, or magical equipment, then distributing resources accordingly. Unlike many classes that focus primarily on optimizing their own turns, artificers frequently make decisions that affect the entire group's performance throughout an adventuring day.

During combat, artificers typically juggle several overlapping responsibilities: supporting allies, managing subclass features, maintaining concentration spells, and contributing damage or control when opportunities arise. Their turns are rarely explosive, but the cumulative impact of good preparation and smart resource allocation can be felt across every encounter. Artificers are also remarkably self-sufficient, able to shift toward offense, defense, support, or utility when circumstances demand it. The class is moderately complex mechanically, but especially rewarding for players who enjoy evaluating tradeoffs, identifying what the party needs most, and adjusting their toolkit to meet those needs.
lore.subclasses: Every artificer approaches problems through magical invention, but each subclass answers a different question about where those inventions should be focused. Some turn themselves into heavily armored combatants, some deploy magical artillery, some fight alongside engineered companions, and others support their party through restorative mixtures and experimental formulas. Unlike many classes where subclasses primarily change combat abilities, artificer subclasses often redefine how you contribute to the group's overall strategy. Whether you're enhancing yourself, empowering allies, controlling space, or commanding constructs, every artificer remains a builder of solutions—but the form those solutions take can be dramatically different.

data.tagline: Fill the battlefield with magical firepower and let your inventions do the talking.
data.overview: The Artillerist focuses on destructive spellcasting supported by magical cannons that provide sustained offense or defensive utility. These arcane weapons can bombard enemies, protect nearby allies, or control space throughout an encounter, allowing the artificer to influence the battlefield every round. It plays like a dedicated magical artillery platform, excelling at ranged combat and area damage while remaining safely behind the front line.

data.tagline: Strike first, strike hard, and end the fight before it truly begins.
data.overview: Assassins specialize in eliminating priority targets before they have a chance to react. Their abilities reward careful planning, stealth, and ambush tactics, making them devastating when they control how an encounter begins. Playing one feels patient and calculated, with your biggest victories coming from preparation rather than prolonged combat.

definingFeature.name: Assassinate
definingFeature.desc: Advantage on surprised foes, and automatic critical hits against them.

data.tagline: Lead from the front, inspiring allies through courage and determination.
data.overview: Bannerets embody the ideal of the battlefield commander, supporting their companions through leadership rather than magic. Their abilities improve the effectiveness of the entire party by allowing allies to recover, reposition, and fight with renewed confidence while the Banneret remains an accomplished Fighter in their own right. Playing one feels collaborative, finding satisfaction in making the entire team stronger through your presence.

data.tagline: A fury-fueled warrior of primal might.
data.overview: Barbarians are warriors who overcome impossible odds through raw physical power and sheer determination. Rather than relying on refined technique or magical abilities, they thrive by charging directly into danger, enduring punishment that would cripple other adventurers, and hitting back even harder. They excel when the fight becomes chaotic, turning aggression into a weapon and surviving encounters through toughness as much as skill. Regardless of specialization, every Barbarian embodies the fantasy of an unstoppable force that refuses to stay down.

definingFeature.name: Rage
definingFeature.desc: Enter a battle fury for bonus damage, resistance to physical damage, and advantage on Strength checks and saves.

lore.role:
Tank • Bruiser • Single-Target DPS

Barbarians are front-line combatants who absorb enemy attention while dealing consistently heavy melee damage. Rather than protecting allies through shields or tactics, they survive by being incredibly difficult to kill, allowing them to pressure dangerous enemies and dominate prolonged fights.
lore.playstyle: Playing a Barbarian is straightforward on the surface but rewards good positioning and target selection. Their turns are often focused on getting into melee quickly and staying there, making them one of the most accessible martial classes for new players. Outside combat, Barbarians tend to solve problems through physicality, intimidation, and endurance rather than subtlety or knowledge, giving them a distinct role during exploration and social encounters.
lore.subclasses: Every Barbarian shares the same foundation of relentless offense and exceptional durability, but subclasses determine how they express that strength. Some become nearly indestructible defenders, others channel supernatural spirits or primal magic, while some focus entirely on overwhelming offense or battlefield disruption. No matter the path, every Barbarian remains a fearless front-line warrior whose greatest strength is meeting danger head-on.

data.tagline: A jack-of-all-trades whose art is magic.
data.overview: Bards are masters of inspiration, creativity, and versatility, weaving magic through music, stories, performances, or sheer force of personality. Rather than specializing in one discipline, they excel at adapting to the needs of the moment—supporting allies, hindering enemies, solving problems, and filling gaps wherever the party needs them most. Their greatest strength isn't raw magical power, but their ability to elevate everyone around them while remaining remarkably capable in almost any situation.

definingFeature.name: Bardic Inspiration
definingFeature.desc: Hand allies a die they can add to a later check, attack, or save — a flexible bonus you grant from across the field.

lore.role:
Support / Controller / Flex

Bards specialize in empowering allies and disrupting enemies through buffs, debuffs, crowd control, and utility magic. While they can contribute respectable damage or even fight on the front lines depending on their subclass, their true value comes from making the entire party perform better and giving the group answers to a wide variety of challenges.
lore.playstyle: Bards reward creativity and adaptability more than rigid optimization. They constantly make small decisions that influence the flow of combat, choosing whether to inspire allies, disable enemies, cast versatile spells, or step into another role as needed. Outside combat, they are among the strongest classes in the game, often excelling in social encounters, investigations, negotiations, performances, and general problem-solving. The class is moderately complex, encouraging players to think beyond simply defeating enemies and instead focus on enabling the entire party to succeed. Getting stronger as a Bard isn't just about level-ups, but learning the broad range of choices while managing your magic resources and being ready to improve through non-combat scenarios.
lore.subclasses: Every bard shares the identity of a charismatic support caster whose magic is tied to expression and inspiration. The subclasses determine how that inspiration manifests. Some become exceptional diplomats, others fearless warriors, storytellers, mediums, manipulators, or creators of magical wonders. While every bard retains their flexible core, each college shifts the balance between support, offense, control, and utility in dramatically different ways.
lore.what_does_the_bard_really_offer:
The bard is one of D&D's most difficult classes to evaluate because much of its strength comes from understanding the situation rather than using a specific ability. While other classes often solve problems directly, the bard excels at identifying where a small push can create the greatest impact. A well-timed Inspiration can turn a missed attack into a victory, a carefully chosen spell can remove the most dangerous enemy from a fight, and a successful conversation can avoid a battle entirely. Because of this, many experienced players describe the bard as a force multiplier—a character whose greatest strength is making everyone else more effective.

The bard rewards players who pay attention to the relationships between the party, the world, and the challenges in front of them. A skilled bard learns where the group's weaknesses are, which allies need support, which enemies pose the greatest threat, and when a social encounter is becoming more dangerous than a combat encounter. Rather than excelling in a single area, the bard moves freely between many roles, adapting to whatever the moment requires. In the hands of a player who understands the flow of the campaign and how their abilities influence the people and events around them, the bard can have an impact on nearly every part of the adventure. Their greatest successes are often subtle, but over time they have a remarkable ability to make difficult situations tilt in the party's favor.

loreLabels.what_does_the_bard_really_offer: What does the Bard Really Offer?

data.tagline: A tactical genius who wins through skill, precision, and battlefield control.
data.overview: Battle Masters study martial techniques that let them influence the flow of combat in ways other Fighters cannot. Their special maneuvers allow them to trip enemies, protect allies, disarm opponents, or create openings through superior technique rather than raw strength. Playing one rewards thoughtful decision-making, with every turn presenting opportunities to outplay your opponents.

data.tagline: Stand beside your greatest invention and fight as one.
data.overview: The Battle Smith blends martial combat with magical support, fighting alongside a loyal mechanical companion that protects allies and pressures enemies. Unlike many support-focused artificers, it is comfortable on the front lines, combining weapon attacks, spellcasting, and coordinated teamwork with its construct. The subclass rewards thoughtful positioning and cooperation, making both the artificer and its companion feel like equal parts of a single fighting unit.

data.tagline: Two combatants. One strategy.
data.overview: Beast Master transforms the ranger into a coordinated fighting pair, where success comes from managing both your own actions and your companion's positioning. Your beast isn't just extra damage—it can block movement, pressure vulnerable enemies, protect allies, and help control the battlefield in ways a lone ranger cannot. The subclass rewards players who enjoy planning around two pieces on the board, constantly deciding whether the ranger or their companion is best suited to solve the immediate problem.

data.tagline: A graceful spellblade who dances between swordplay and spellcasting.
data.overview: Bladesingers blend elegant melee combat with arcane magic, becoming surprisingly agile and resilient for a Wizard. Rather than standing safely behind the front line, they weave spells and weapon attacks together while relying on speed and precision instead of heavy armor. Playing one feels fast and fluid, rewarding careful positioning and balancing offense with defense.

data.tagline: The classic infernal line.
data.overview: The tieflings connected to Nessus command the power of fire and darkness, guided by a keener than normal intellect, as befits those linked to Asmodeus himself.

definingFeature.name: Infernal Legacy
definingFeature.desc: Thaumaturgy, then Hellish Rebuke and Darkness.

data.tagline: Lord of decay.
data.overview: The crumbling realm of Maladomini is ruled by Baalzebul, who excels at corrupting those whose minor sins can be transformed into acts of damnation. Tieflings linked to this archdevil can corrupt others both physically and psychically.

definingFeature.name: Legacy of Maladomini
definingFeature.desc: Thaumaturgy, then Ray of Sickness and Crown of Madness.

data.tagline: Master of secrets.
data.overview: The great city of Dis occupies most of Hell's second layer. It is a place where secrets are uncovered and shared with the highest bidder, making tieflings tied to Dispater excellent spies and infiltrators.

definingFeature.name: Legacy of Dis
definingFeature.desc: Thaumaturgy, then Disguise Self and Detect Thoughts.

data.tagline: Line of influence.
data.overview: A master manipulator, Fierna grants tieflings tied to her forceful personalities.

definingFeature.name: Legacy of Phlegethos
definingFeature.desc: Friends, then Charm Person and Suggestion.

data.tagline: Line of trickery.
data.overview: Glasya, Hell's criminal mastermind, grants her tiefiings magic that is useful for committing heists.

definingFeature.name: Legacy of Malbolge
definingFeature.desc: Minor Illusion, then Disguise Self and Invisibility.

data.tagline: Line of ice.
data.overview: Frozen Stygia is ruled by Levistus, an archdevil known for offering bargains to those who face an inescapable doom.

definingFeature.name: Legacy of Stygia
definingFeature.desc: Ray of Frost, then Armor of Agathys and Darkness.

data.tagline: Line of greed.
data.overview: The great miser Mammon loves coins above all else. Tieflings tied to him excel at gathering and safeguarding wealth.

definingFeature.name: Legacy of Minauros
definingFeature.desc: Mage Hand, then Tenser's Floating Disk and Arcane Lock.

data.tagline: Line of arcane fire.
data.overview: In the frozen realm of Cania, Mephistopheles offers arcane power to those who entreat with him. Tieflings linked to him master some arcane magic.

definingFeature.name: Legacy of Cania
definingFeature.desc: Mage Hand, then Burning Hands and Flame Blade.

data.tagline: Line of war.
data.overview: Tieflings with a blood tie to Zariel are stronger than the typical tiefling and receive magical abilities that aid them in battle.

definingFeature.name: Legacy of Avernus
definingFeature.desc: Thaumaturgy, then Searing Smite and Branding Smite.

data.tagline: The steadfast guardian who protects allies and refuses to yield ground.
data.overview: Cavaliers excel at defending companions and punishing enemies who ignore them. While often associated with mounted combat, their protective abilities remain highly effective even on foot, making them exceptional front-line defenders in nearly any campaign. Playing one feels like holding the line, forcing enemies to deal with you before they can threaten anyone else.

data.tagline: Simple, reliable, and brutally effective—the pure martial warrior.
data.overview: The Champion focuses on refining the Fighter's core strengths rather than introducing complex mechanics. It emphasizes athletic excellence, durability, and consistently powerful attacks, making it one of the easiest subclasses to pick up while remaining effective throughout an entire campaign. Playing a Champion feels dependable—you spend less time managing abilities and more time mastering the fundamentals of positioning and combat.

data.tagline: Bend time itself to ensure events unfold in your favor.
data.overview: Chronurgy Wizards manipulate the flow of time, altering initiative, delaying effects, and influencing the outcome of crucial moments. Their abilities reward foresight and tactical planning, allowing them to reshape encounters in subtle but incredibly powerful ways. Playing one feels like quietly rewriting history, ensuring the battle progresses exactly as you intended.

data.tagline: Keep your allies standing while the wilderness quietly works in your favor.
data.overview: The Circle of Dreams leans heavily into healing, protection, and keeping the party moving through long adventures. Its healing doesn't compete directly with its spellcasting, allowing you to rescue injured allies without giving up your turn's biggest spell. Much of the gameplay revolves around deciding whether to spend your resources preventing the next problem or saving them for when things inevitably go wrong.

data.tagline: Life and death are part of the same cycle—use both as your weapons.
data.overview: Circle of Spores encourages druids to fight much closer to the action than most of their peers. Rather than transforming into animals, it often uses Wild Shape to awaken a cloud of deadly spores that punishes nearby enemies while making the druid far tougher. The subclass constantly asks whether it's worth stepping into dangerous positions to maximize its abilities or staying back to preserve concentration on powerful control spells.

definingFeature.name: Symbiotic Entity
definingFeature.desc: Spend Wild Shape for temporary hit points and supercharged spore damage.

data.tagline: The heavens always have another answer if you're willing to read them.
data.overview: Circle of Stars adds remarkable consistency to the druid's toolkit by letting you shift between offensive, defensive, and supportive celestial forms without giving up access to your spellcasting. Before or during a fight, you'll regularly choose which stellar form best fits the encounter—more damage, better healing, or stronger concentration on your spells. It rewards players who enjoy adapting from round to round instead of committing to a single role.

definingFeature.name: Starry Form
definingFeature.desc: Spend Wild Shape to take a constellation form granting ranged attacks, healing, or insight.

data.tagline: Sometimes nature protects by letting the old burn away to make room for the new.
data.overview: Circle of Wildfire balances destruction and restoration, pairing explosive fire magic with healing and a loyal wildfire spirit that fights beside you. You'll constantly decide whether to use the spirit to improve your mobility, support allies, or help pressure enemies while choosing between offensive fire spells and the druid's broader support toolkit. It captures the dual nature of fire: equally capable of devastation and renewal.

Circle of the Land doubles down on the druid's identity as a full spellcaster, expanding both its magical endurance and the variety of spells it can bring to an adventure. Rather than relying heavily on Wild Shape in combat, it rewards careful spell selection and recognizing which terrain-controlling or utility spell will have the biggest impact. If you enjoy solving problems through clever spell choice, this is the quintessential caster druid.
data.tagline: Master the land itself, and you'll always have the right spell prepared.
When the fighting starts, become the beast instead of commanding one.
data.overview: Circle of the Moon transforms Wild Shape from a utility tool into your primary combat feature. You'll regularly decide whether a situation is better solved with powerful spellcasting or by immediately becoming a bear, wolf, elemental, or another powerful beast and fighting on the front line. It offers one of the most durable playstyles in the game, rewarding players who know exactly when to switch between animal instincts and magical mastery.

data.tagline: The battlefield belongs to those who never fight alone.
data.overview: Circle of the Shepherd specializes in empowering summoned creatures and supporting the entire party through powerful spiritual totems. Positioning becomes especially important, both for your allies and for the creatures you've summoned, as much of your effectiveness comes from keeping everyone within the right areas of influence. Success often comes from coordinating multiple friendly creatures rather than maximizing your own individual actions.

data.tagline: A divine conduit of healing and judgment.
data.overview: Clerics are divine spellcasters whose magic comes from their faith and devotion to a higher power. They wield some of the most versatile magic in the game, able to heal wounds, protect allies, destroy undead, reshape the battlefield, and call down devastating divine wrath. Their connection to a deity also makes them one of the classes most naturally tied to a world's religions, politics, and history, giving plenty of opportunities for roleplaying beyond combat.

definingFeature.name: Channel Divinity
definingFeature.desc: Spend divine energy on Turn Undead or a domain power, recharging on a short rest.

lore.role:
Support / Healer / Controller / Flex

Clerics are the backbone of many adventuring parties. They can deal impressive damage—especially against undead—but one of their biggest challenges is deciding when to spend their turn supporting the team instead. Every round asks the question: Is ending the fight faster more valuable than keeping an ally standing? Knowing when to attack, heal, protect, or disable enemies is what separates an average cleric from an exceptional one.
lore.playstyle: Clerics are mechanically straightforward to learn but difficult to master. They have access to a wide range of powerful spells each day, so much of their skill comes from choosing the right tools before an adventure and recognizing the right moment to use them. Unlike dedicated healers in many RPGs, clerics are expected to actively contribute throughout a fight rather than stand in the back casting healing spells. Some battles call for divine fire, others for protective blessings or timely healing, and learning to shift between those roles is the essence of playing a cleric well.
lore.subclasses: Every cleric draws from the same powerful divine spell list, but each Divine Domain changes what your deity expects of you and how you serve them. Some domains encourage fighting alongside the front line, others emphasize preserving life, gathering forgotten knowledge, crafting divine relics, or even wielding death itself. The subclass shapes not only your combat style, but often your place within the world's religions and your relationship with the people who share—or oppose—your faith.

data.tagline: Bring perfect order to a chaotic world and make chance itself obey your will.
data.overview: Clockwork Soul focuses on balance, protection, and consistency, counteracting randomness while shielding allies from harm. Its expanded magical options allow it to control the battlefield, reinforce companions, and solve problems with remarkable reliability. Rather than producing dramatic bursts of chaos, this subclass shines by steadily imposing order and ensuring the party performs at its best regardless of the situation.

data.tagline: Bring imagination to life and let your creations shape the battlefield.
data.overview: The College of Creation turns artistic expression into tangible magic, allowing the bard to conjure objects, animate items, and solve problems with physical manifestations of creativity. It thrives on flexibility, creating opportunities that other classes simply cannot. Playing one rewards inventive thinking, as the best solution is often something no one else considered.

data.tagline: Words become weapons when every sentence is impossible to ignore.
data.overview: The College of Eloquence focuses on persuasion, diplomacy, and magical influence, becoming one of the most reliable support bards available. It excels at ensuring allies succeed while making enemy resistance increasingly difficult. Whether negotiating peace or dismantling an opponent's resolve, an Eloquence bard succeeds through confidence and precision rather than spectacle.

data.tagline: Command every room as though the world itself wants to follow your lead.
data.overview: The College of Glamour channels captivating supernatural beauty into powerful support abilities that reposition allies and influence crowds. It shines by protecting teammates, controlling the flow of battle, and inspiring others to perform beyond their limits. The subclass feels like a charismatic leader whose presence alone reshapes the encounter.

data.tagline: Knowledge is your greatest performance, and every secret is another tool.
data.overview: The College of Lore embraces the bard's role as the ultimate generalist, expanding its magical options and unmatched versatility. It excels at adapting to nearly any challenge, often borrowing techniques that normally belong to entirely different classes. Playing a Lore bard feels like always having another answer waiting in reserve, making it one of the most flexible subclasses in the game.

data.tagline: Every tale carries power, especially those told by the dead.
data.overview: The College of Spirits draws upon ghostly legends and supernatural storytellers to produce unpredictable but powerful magical effects. It blends support, utility, and battlefield control with a strong element of chance, making each encounter feel slightly different from the last. The subclass rewards players who enjoy embracing narrative flavor alongside mechanical variety.

data.tagline: Turn every duel into a performance where style is just as deadly as skill.
data.overview: The College of Swords transforms the bard into an agile martial combatant who mixes weapon attacks with graceful magical flourishes. Rather than standing behind the front line, it actively weaves between offense, defense, and mobility while still providing support when needed. The result is a highly active playstyle that rewards positioning and tactical movement.

data.tagline: Lead from the front and inspire courage where it matters most.
data.overview: The College of Valor is built for bards who want to stand shoulder to shoulder with the party's warriors. It combines durable frontline combat with classic bardic support, encouraging a balance between weapon attacks, protective magic, and inspiring allies. Compared to the more agile College of Swords, Valor emphasizes teamwork and resilience over personal flair.

data.tagline: Fear, secrets, and manipulation are sharper than any blade.
data.overview: The College of Whispers specializes in deception, psychological warfare, and devastating single-target strikes. It uses fear and hidden knowledge to undermine enemies both socially and in combat, making it equally dangerous during negotiations and assassinations. Playing a Whispers bard feels like always operating one step beneath the surface, where information and intimidation become your most powerful weapons.

data.tagline: Drow of the Underdark.
data.overview:
Descended from an earlier subrace of dark-skinned elves, the drow were banished from the surface world for following the goddess Lolth down the path to evil and corruption. Now they have built their own civilization in the depths of the Underdark, patterned after the Way of Lolth. Also called dark elves. The drow have black skin that resembles polished obsidian and stark white or pale yellow hair. They commonly have very pale eyes (so pale as to be mistaken for white) in shades of lilac, silver, pink, red, and blue. They tend to be smaller and thinner than most elves.

Drow adventurers are rare, and the race does not exist in all worlds. Check with your Dungeon Master to see if you can play a drow character.

definingFeature.name: Drow Magic
definingFeature.desc: Superior darkvision plus Dancing Lights, Faerie Fire, and Darkness.

lore.society: Surface dwellers often fear or revile drow on sight thanks to their reputation; many drow adventurers struggle against that prejudice while forging their own path.

data.tagline: If death comes for your enemies, make sure it arrives quickly.
data.overview: Death clerics trade much of the traditional support identity for raw offensive magic. They specialize in necrotic damage, punishing weakened enemies, and eliminating threats before they can overwhelm the party. Playing one means constantly deciding whether removing an enemy from the fight is worth more than spending that turn protecting an ally—a much more aggressive mindset than most clerics adopt.

data.tagline: Blend arcane talent with divine grace to become a master of both offense and support.
data.overview: Divine Soul dramatically expands the sorcerer's options by granting access to powerful restorative and protective magic alongside traditional arcane spells. It remains an exceptional offensive caster while gaining the ability to heal allies, remove harmful effects, and strengthen the party. The result is one of the most versatile spellcasters in the game, capable of filling multiple team roles without sacrificing magical power.

data.tagline: Channel the majesty of dragons and wield elemental power with unwavering confidence.
data.overview: Draconic Bloodline grants the sorcerer greater resilience than most spellcasters while strengthening spells tied to a chosen dragon's elemental affinity. It blends durability with reliable damage, allowing the sorcerer to stay in dangerous situations longer than expected. The subclass feels like a classic battle mage whose magical heritage grows more evident as their power increases.

data.tagline: Proud dragon-blooded humanoids with a breath weapon.
data.overview:
Born of dragons, as their name proclaims, the dragonborn walk proudly through a world that greets them with fearful incomprehension. Shaped by draconic gods or the dragons themselves, dragonborn originally hatched from dragon eggs as a unique race, combining the best attributes of dragons and humanoids. Some dragonborn are faithful servants to true dragons, others form the ranks of soldiers in great wars, and still others find themselves adrift, with no clear calling in life.

They are distantly related to a particular kind of dragon. Their ancestry determines the damage and area of their breath weapon, and the type of resistance they have.

definingFeature.name: Breath Weapon
definingFeature.desc: Exhale a line or cone of elemental damage determined by your draconic ancestry.

lore.lifespan: Young dragonborn grow quickly. They walk hours after hatching, attain the size and development of a 10-year-old human child by the age of 3, and reach adulthood by 15. They live to be around 80.
lore.society: Value clan and personal honor above all; many wander as exiles or mercenaries proving their worth.
lore.physical: Dragonborn are taller and heavier than humans, standing well over 6 feet tall and averaging almost 250 pounds. Their size is Medium.
lore.alignment: Dragonborn tend towards extremes, making a conscious choice for one side or the other between Good and Evil (represented by Bahamut and Tiamat, respectively). More side with Bahamut than Tiamat (whose non-dragon followers are mostly kobolds), but villainous dragonborn can be quite terrible indeed. Some rare few choose to devote themselves to lesser dragon deities, such as Chronepsis (Neutral), and fewer still choose to worship Io, the Ninefold Dragon, who is all alignments at once.

data.tagline: Raise a dragon. Fight as one.
data.overview: Drakewarden builds around a draconic companion that steadily grows alongside you, becoming a larger part of your strategy as you level. Unlike Beast Master, the drake leans more toward durability and direct combat support, adding elemental damage, mobility, and eventually aerial options to the ranger's toolkit. The subclass feels like a gradual partnership, rewarding players who enjoy watching a companion evolve from a helpful ally into a defining feature of their combat style.

data.tagline: A shapeshifting guardian of the wild.
data.overview:
Druids are guardians of the natural world, wielding primal magic drawn from the land itself. They command one of the broadest collections of spells in the game, capable of dealing devastating damage, healing allies, controlling the battlefield, summoning creatures, or protecting the party from the elements. Their signature ability, Wild Shape, adds another layer entirely, allowing them to abandon spellcasting altogether and take the form of beasts to scout, infiltrate, explore, or fight. A druid can freeze an advancing army with a blizzard, restore the party's strength, then become a bear and charge into the front line—all within the same encounter.

Beyond combat, druids excel in exploration. Wild Shape allows them to slip into enemy camps as a mouse, scout from above as a hawk, swim beneath dangerous waters, or traverse terrain that would stop most adventurers. Their connection to nature lets them understand wildlife, survive in hostile environments, and uncover clues others would overlook. That deep bond with the natural world also shapes their roleplaying, as many druids see themselves as protectors of balance rather than conquerors of it.

definingFeature.name: Wild Shape
definingFeature.desc: Assume the form of a beast you have seen, gaining its statistics for exploration, scouting, or a tankier body in a fight.

lore.role:
Controller / Support / Flex

Druids are among the most adaptable classes in D&D. They can lock down large sections of the battlefield with terrain and hazards, provide powerful healing and support, unleash respectable magical damage, or transform into durable frontline combatants. Their biggest challenge isn't lacking options—it's deciding which role the party needs most at any given moment.
lore.playstyle: Druids are easy to enjoy but difficult to master. At a basic level, you can always contribute by casting reliable spells or using Wild Shape to survive dangerous situations. As you gain experience, however, the class opens dramatically. Every encounter asks questions like: Should I spend this fight concentrating on battlefield control? Is this the moment to heal? Would turning into an animal accomplish more than casting another spell? Should I save Wild Shape for scouting after the battle? Experienced druids succeed not because they always have the strongest ability, but because they recognize which of their many tools best fits the situation.
lore.subclasses: Every druid shares the same foundation of primal spellcasting and Wild Shape, but each Circle emphasizes a different expression of nature. Some strengthen beast transformations, others focus on summoning allies, healing companions, commanding stars, spreading decay, or wielding destructive elemental forces. Rather than replacing the druid's flexibility, most subclasses encourage you to lean into one aspect of the class while still leaving the rest of its versatile toolkit available.
lore.decide_what_kind_of_druid_you_want_to_be: The druid is one of the most flexible classes in D&D, but that flexibility can also be its greatest challenge. Between your spell list, Wild Shape, and subclass, you have access to healing, battlefield control, exploration, utility, scouting, and, depending on your Circle, even front-line combat. The key is understanding that no single druid excels equally at all of these roles. Before choosing the class, think less about everything a druid can do, and more about what you want to do. Your subclass will heavily influence your strengths, while your spell preparation allows you to adapt around them. Druids thrive when they have a clear identity, even if that identity changes from one adventure to the next.
lore.flexibility_is_your_greatest_strength: Unlike many classes, a druid can adjust their prepared spells after every Long Rest, allowing them to respond to the challenges ahead or fill gaps within the party. This adaptability makes the class incredibly rewarding for players who enjoy planning ahead and thinking creatively. It also means you'll often have more choices than almost anyone else at the table. Rather than trying to prepare a little bit of everything, experienced druids usually focus on the role their party needs most that day, trusting their natural versatility to handle unexpected situations as they arise. The druid isn't powerful because it always has the perfect answer—it's powerful because it can become the right character for the adventure ahead.

data.tagline: Stout, stalwart folk of stone and forge.
data.overview: Kingdoms rich in ancient grandeur, halls carved into the roots of mountains, the echoing of picks and hammers in deep mines and blazing forges, a commitment to clan and tradition, and a burning hatred of goblins and orcs – these common threads unite all dwarves.

definingFeature.name: Dwarven Resilience
definingFeature.desc: Advantage on saves against poison and resistance to poison damage, plus darkvision and stonecunning.

lore.lifespan: Dwarves mature at the same rate as humans, but they're considered young until they reach the age of 50. On average, they live about 350 years.
lore.society: Clan- and craft-focused, valuing tradition, loyalty, and grudges held for generations. Mountain holds and deep mines are home.
lore.physical: Short and broad (4–5 ft) but heavy, with thick hair and prized beards.
lore.alignment: Most dwarves are lawful, believing firmly in the benefits of a well-ordered society. They tend toward good as well, with a strong sense of fair play and a belief that everyone deserves to share in the benefits of a just order.

data.tagline: Steady as stone.
data.overview:
Tracing their ancestry to dao, the genies of the Elemental Plane of Earth, earth genasi inherit dao’s steadfast strength and control over earth.

An earth genasi’s skin can be the colors of stone and earth or a human skin tone with glittering sparkles like gem dust. Some earth genasi have lines marking their skin like cracks, either showing glimmering gemlike veins or a dim, yellowish glow. Earth genasi hair can appear carved of stone or crystal or resemble strands of spun metal.

definingFeature.name: Merge with Stone
definingFeature.desc: Unimpeded movement over difficult earthen terrain; Pass Without Trace at higher level.

data.tagline: Fight alongside a spectral duplicate that turns the battlefield into your domain.
data.overview: Echo Knights summon a mystical echo of themselves that can attack, defend, and occupy space independently. This unique mechanic allows them to threaten enemies from multiple positions at once, creating opportunities for clever movement, defense, and offense unlike any other Fighter. Playing one feels highly tactical, constantly thinking about positioning—not just for yourself, but for your echo as well.

data.tagline: A warrior who reinforces steel with arcane magic.
data.overview: Eldritch Knights combine traditional martial combat with a carefully chosen selection of wizard spells. Their magic enhances their survivability, mobility, and versatility without replacing their role as front-line Fighters, allowing them to solve problems that ordinary warriors cannot. Playing one feels like weaving spells seamlessly between weapon strikes, balancing martial discipline with magical utility.

data.tagline: Graceful, long-lived folk attuned to magic.
data.overview: Elves are a magical people of otherworldly grace, living in places of ethereal beauty, in the midst of ancient forests or in silvery spires glittering with faerie light, where soft music drifts through the air and gentle fragrances waft on the breeze. Elves love nature and magic, art and artistry, music and poetry.

definingFeature.name: Fey Ancestry & Trance
definingFeature.desc: Advantage against being charmed, immunity to magical sleep, and a 4-hour meditative trance in place of sleep.

lore.lifespan: Although elves reach physical maturity at about the same age as humans, the elven understanding of adulthood goes beyond physical growth to encompass worldly experience. An elf typically claims adulthood and an adult name around the age of 100 and can live to be 750 years old.
lore.society: Reclusive and refined, viewing shorter-lived races as hasty. Communities prize art, magic, and long memory.
lore.physical: Slender and a little shorter than humans, with pointed ears and an ageless, otherworldly grace.
lore.alignment: Elves love freedom, variety, and self-expression, so they lean strongly towards the gentler aspects of chaos. They value and protect others' freedom as well as their own, and are good more often than not. Drow are an exception; their exile into the Underdark has made them vicious and dangerous. Drow are more often evil than not.

data.tagline: Tiny winged folk of the Feywild.
data.overview:
The Feywild is home to many fantastic peoples, including fairies. Fairies are a wee folk, but not nearly as much so as their pixie and sprite friends. The first fairies spoke Elvish, Goblin, or Sylvan, and encounters with human visitors prompted many of them to learn Common as well.

Infused with the magic of the Feywild, most fairies look like Small elves with insectile wings, but each fairy has a special physical characteristic that sets the fairy apart. For your fairy, roll on the Fey Characteristics table or choose an option from it. You’re also free to come up with your own characteristic if none of the suggestions below fit your character.

definingFeature.name: Fairy Magic & Flight
definingFeature.desc: Innate fey spells and a flying speed from gossamer wings.

lore.lifespan: Long-lived like their fey kin, often centuries.
lore.society: Whimsical and curious, tied to the courts and wild magic of the Feywild.
lore.physical: Small (around 3 ft) and delicate, with insect- or leaf-like wings.

data.tagline: Charm your allies, unsettle your enemies.
data.overview: Fey Wanderer blends martial combat with the unpredictable influence of the Feywild, adding social presence and subtle magical control to the ranger's toolkit. Rather than focusing solely on damage, it excels at redirecting fear and charm effects, manipulating enemy behavior, and remaining effective in both conversations and combat. Players who enjoy alternating between diplomacy, utility, and reliable weapon attacks will appreciate having meaningful contributions in almost every pillar of the game.

data.tagline: The master of weapons and war.
data.overview: Fighters are masters of martial combat, defined not by a single fighting style but by their ability to excel with almost any weapon, armor, or battlefield role. Through discipline, training, and experience, they become adaptable warriors capable of meeting nearly any challenge head-on. Whether standing as an unbreakable defender, a deadly archer, or an aggressive duelist, Fighters succeed through consistency and versatility rather than supernatural gifts. Regardless of specialization, every Fighter represents the pinnacle of skill earned through dedication and practice.

definingFeature.name: Action Surge
definingFeature.desc: Take an entire extra action on your turn, then recharge on a short rest — burst potential no other class matches.

lore.role:
Flex • Tank • Bruiser • Single-Target DPS
Due to the array of subclasses, the Fighter is one of the most adaptable classes in the game, capable of filling almost any martial role depending on their equipment and subclass. They can defend allies, deal sustained damage, control enemy movement, or specialize in ranged combat, making them valuable in nearly every party composition.
lore.playstyle: Playing a Fighter rewards solid tactical decision-making rather than complex resource management. They consistently perform well in combat, with the flexibility to adapt to changing situations and make effective use of weapons, positioning, and teamwork. While Fighters are often most comfortable in battle, many subclasses expand their capabilities with leadership, magic, or unique utility that gives them meaningful contributions outside combat as well.
lore.subclasses:
Every Fighter shares the same foundation of martial excellence, but subclasses determine how that mastery is expressed. Some focus on perfecting technique, others blend swordplay with magic or supernatural power, while others become inspiring leaders, mounted warriors, or masters of battlefield tactics. No matter the path, every Fighter is defined by skill, reliability, and the ability to rise to whatever challenge lies ahead.
Each fighter branch has a very distinct feel that separates it from the other sub classes allowing for different fighters to have very different roles and playstyles.

data.tagline: Hot-blooded and bright.
data.overview: Descended from efreet, the genies of the Elemental Plane of Fire, fire genasi channel the flamboyant and often destructive nature of flame. They show their heritage in their skin tones, which can range from deep charcoal to shades of red and orange. Some bear skin tones common to humanity but with fiery marks, such as slowly swirling lights under their skin that resemble embers or glowing red lines tracing over their bodies like cracks. Fire genasi hair can resemble threads of fire or sooty smoke.

definingFeature.name: Reach to the Blaze
definingFeature.desc: Darkvision and fire resistance; Produce Flame and Burning Hands.

data.tagline: Shy and illusion-touched.
data.overview: As a forest gnome, you have a natural knack for illusion and inherent quickness and stealth. In the worlds of D&D, forest gnomes are rare and secretive. They gather in hidden communities in sylvan forests, using illusions and trickery to conceal themselves from threats or to mask their escape should they be detected. Forest gnomes tend to be friendly with other good-spirited woodland folk, and they regard elves and good fey as their most important allies. These gnomes also befriend small forest animals and rely on them for information about threats that might prowl their lands.

definingFeature.name: Natural Illusionist
definingFeature.desc: You know the Minor Illusion cantrip and can speak with small animals.

data.tagline: Stand where the fighting is fiercest, clad in armor blessed by your own handiwork.
data.overview: Forge clerics become durable frontline defenders without sacrificing their spellcasting. They strengthen weapons and armor, shrug off punishment, and comfortably alternate between swinging a weapon and casting powerful support spells. Much of the gameplay revolves around choosing when to stand beside your party's warriors and when to step back to solve problems only a spellcaster can.

data.tagline: Elemental-touched humanoids of the planes.
data.overview: Tracing their ancestry to the genies of the Elemental Planes, each genasi can tap into the power of one of the elements. Air, earth, fire, and water — these are the four pillars of the Material Plane and the four types of genasi. Some genasi are direct descendants of a genie, while others were born to non-genasi parents who lived near a place suffused by a genie’s magic.

definingFeature.name: Elemental Heritage
definingFeature.desc: Resistances and innate spells determined by your elemental variant.

lore.society: Rare and varied; many feel pulled toward the element in their blood and the planes it ties them to.
lore.physical: Human-like with an elemental tell — flame-like hair, crystalline skin, a constant breeze, or watery sheen.

data.tagline: Strike from the darkness before the battle truly begins.
data.overview: Gloom Stalker specializes in opening fights with overwhelming momentum. Its abilities emphasize acting early, remaining difficult to detect, and eliminating dangerous targets before they have an opportunity to respond. While the subclass remains effective throughout longer encounters, it shines brightest when preparation, stealth, and target selection allow it to dictate the first moments of combat. It's an excellent choice for players who enjoy planning engagements and capitalizing on the advantage of going first.

data.tagline: Small, brilliant, irrepressibly curious folk.
data.overview: A constant hum of busy activity pervades the warrens and neighborhoods where gnomes form their close-knit communities. Louder sounds punctuate the hum: a crunch of grinding gears here, a minor explosion there, a yelp of surprise or triumph, and especially bursts of laughter. Gnomes take delight in life, enjoying every moment of invention, exploration, investigation, creation, and play.

definingFeature.name: Gnome Cunning
definingFeature.desc: Advantage on Intelligence, Wisdom, and Charisma saving throws against magic.

lore.lifespan: Gnomes mature at the same rate as humans, and most are expected to settle into adult life around the age of 40. They can live to 350 years on average, but it's not too uncommon for them to reach 500 years of age.
lore.society: Inventive and joyful, delighting in tinkering, illusion, and discovery.
lore.physical: Gnomes are between 3 and 4 feet tall and weigh around 40 pounds. Your size is Small.
lore.alignment: Gnomes are generally Good. Those who tend towards Law are sages, engineers, researchers, scholars, investigators, or inventors. Those who tend towards Chaos are often minstrels, tricksters, wanderers, or fanciful jewelers. Gnomes are light-hearted, and even the tricksters amongst them favor harmless pranks over vicious schemes.

data.tagline: Towering, competitive mountain dwellers.
data.overview: The first goliaths lived on the highest mountain peaks — far above the tree line, where the air is thin and frigid winds howl. Distantly related to giants and infused with the supernatural essence of their ancestors’ mountainous home, goliaths stand between 7 and 8 feet tall and have a wide array of skin tones resembling different types of stone.

definingFeature.name: Stone's Endurance
definingFeature.desc: Reduce incoming damage with a reaction, a number of times per rest tied to your Constitution.

lore.society: Nomadic, intensely competitive, and meritocratic — every member must pull their weight.
lore.physical: Huge (7–8 ft) and powerfully built, with grey, stony skin marked by lithoderm patterns.

data.tagline: Keep your allies alive until fate truly says their time has come.
data.overview: Grave clerics excel at preventing death rather than constantly restoring lost health. They are rewarded for waiting until healing is truly needed instead of topping off every scratch, allowing them to spend more turns controlling the battlefield or helping eliminate dangerous enemies. Their abilities also make them exceptionally effective against undead, reinforcing their role as guardians of the natural cycle of life and death.

data.tagline: Control the battlefield by bending the force of gravity itself.
data.overview: Graviturgy Wizards manipulate weight, momentum, and gravitational forces to hinder enemies and assist allies. Rather than dealing overwhelming damage, they excel at repositioning creatures, limiting movement, and subtly influencing the terrain of combat. Playing one feels highly tactical, constantly reshaping how everyone moves across the battlefield.

data.tagline: Charismatic wanderers of two worlds.
data.overview: Walking in two worlds but truly belonging to neither, half-elves combine what some say are the best qualities of their elf and human parents: human curiosity, inventiveness, and ambition tempered by the refined senses, love of nature, and artistic tastes of the elves.

definingFeature.name: Fey Ancestry & Versatility
definingFeature.desc: Advantage against charm, immunity to magical sleep, and flexible skill and ability bonuses.

lore.lifespan: Half-elves age at much the same rate as humans, reaching adulthood at the age of 20. They live much longer than humans, however, often exceeding 180 years.
lore.society: Often outsiders bridging cultures; valued as diplomats, leaders, and free spirits.
lore.physical: Half-elves are more or less the same size as humans, ranging from 5 to 6 feet tall. Your size is Medium.
lore.alignment: Half-elves share the chaotic bent of their elven heritage. They both value personal freedom and creative expression, demonstrating neither love of leaders nor desire for followers. They chafe at rules, resent others' demands, and sometimes prove unreliable, or at least unpredictable. They are good and evil in equal numbers, a trait they share with their human parents.

data.tagline: Fierce, enduring folk of orcish strength.
data.overview: When alliances between humans and orcs are sealed by marriages, half-orcs are born. Some half-orcs rise to become proud chiefs of orc tribes, their human blood giving them an edge over their full-blooded orc rivals. Some venture into the world to prove their worth among humans and other more civilized races. Many of these become adventurers, achieving greatness for their mighty deeds and notoriety for their barbaric customs and savage fury.

definingFeature.name: Relentless Endurance
definingFeature.desc: Once per long rest, drop to 1 hit point instead of 0 — and Savage Attacks add a die to your critical hits.

lore.lifespan: Half-orcs mature a little faster than humans, reaching adulthood around age 14. They age noticeably faster and rarely live longer than 75 years.
lore.society: Often caught between human and orc worlds; prove themselves through strength and loyalty.
lore.physical: Half-orcs are somewhat larger and bulkier than humans, and they range from 5 to well over 6 feet tall. Your size is Medium.
lore.alignment: Half-orcs inherit a tendency toward chaos from their orc parents and are not strongly inclined toward good. Half-orcs raised among orcs and willing to live out their lives among them are usually evil.

data.tagline: Small, brave, and improbably lucky folk.
data.overview: The comforts of home are the goals of most halflings' lives: a place to settle in peace and quiet, far from marauding monsters and clashing armies. Others form nomadic bands that travel constantly, lured by the open road and the wide horizon to discover the wonders of new lands and peoples. Halflings work readily with others, and they are loyal to their friends, whether halfling or otherwise. They can display remarkable ferocity when their friends, families, or communities are threatened.

definingFeature.name: Lucky & Brave
definingFeature.desc: Reroll natural 1s on d20 tests and gain advantage against being frightened.

lore.lifespan: A halfling reaches adulthood at the age of 20 and generally lives into the middle of his or her second century.
lore.society: Community- and comfort-loving, valuing family, food, and home over ambition.
lore.physical: Halflings average about 3 feet tall and weigh about 40 pounds. Your size is small.
lore.alignment: Most halflings are lawful good. As a rule, they are good-hearted and kind, hate to see others in pain, and have no tolerance for oppression. They are also very orderly and traditional, leaning heavily on the support of their community and the comfort of the old ways.

data.tagline: Scholarly and arcane.
data.overview:
As a high elf, you have a keen mind and a mastery of at least the basics of magic. In many of the worlds of D&D, there are two kinds of high elves. One type (which includes the gray elves and valley elves of Greyhawk, the Silvanesti of Dragonlance, and the sun elves of the Forgotten Realms) is haughty and reclusive, believing themselves to be superior to non-elves and even other elves. The other type (including the high elves of Greyhawk. the Qualinesti of Dragonlance, and the moon elves of the Forgotten Realms) are more common and more friendly, and often encountered among humans and other races.

The sun elves of Faerun (also called gold elves or sunrise elves) have bronze skin and hair of copper, black, or golden blood. Their eyes are golden, silver, or black. Moon elves (also called silver elves or gray elves) are much paler, with alabaster skin sometimes tinged with blue. They often have hair of silver-while, black, or blue, but various shades of blond, brown, and red are not uncommon. Their eyes are blue or green and flecked with gold.

definingFeature.name: Cantrip
definingFeature.desc: You know one wizard cantrip (Intelligence-based).

data.tagline: Wise, hardy, and tough.
data.overview: As a hill dwarf, you have keen senses, deep intuition, and remarkable resilience. The gold dwarves of Faerun in their mighty southern kingdom are hill dwarves, as are the exiled Neidar and the debased Klar of Krynn in the Dragonlance setting.

definingFeature.name: Dwarven Toughness
definingFeature.desc: +1 hit point per level.

data.tagline: No distance is too great. No plane is beyond your reach.
data.overview: Horizon Walker expands the ranger into a highly mobile hunter of extraplanar threats, adding teleportation and force-infused attacks that let it reposition constantly throughout combat. Rather than simply staying at range, this subclass encourages moving aggressively between enemies to pressure whichever target matters most. As it develops, mobility becomes just as important as damage, rewarding players who enjoy thinking about where they should be standing every turn rather than settling into one position for an entire fight.

data.tagline: Ambitious, adaptable, and everywhere.
data.overview: In the reckonings of most worlds, humans are the youngest of the common races, late to arrive on the world scene and short-lived in comparison to dwarves, elves, and dragons. Perhaps it is because of their shorter lives that they strive to achieve as much as they can in the years they are given. Or maybe they feel they have something to prove to the elder races, and that's why they build their mighty empires on the foundation of conquest and trade. Whatever drives them, humans are the innovators, the achievers, and the pioneers of the worlds.

definingFeature.name: Versatility
definingFeature.desc: Broad ability bonuses (or, as a variant, a feat and a skill) make humans fit any class.

lore.lifespan: Humans reach adulthood in their late teens and live less than a century.
lore.society: Endlessly diverse — humans build the widest range of cultures, nations, and faiths.
lore.physical: Highly varied in size, coloration, and feature across every land.
lore.alignment: Humans tend toward no particular alignment. The best and the worst are found among them.

data.tagline: Master the fundamentals until every hunt has an answer.
data.overview: Hunter refines the ranger's core strengths without locking it into a single gimmick or companion. As you level, you'll repeatedly choose from specialized features that improve your effectiveness against groups, powerful single enemies, or specific battlefield situations, allowing you to tailor the subclass to your preferred style. Rather than dramatically changing how the ranger plays, Hunter reinforces the class's identity as an adaptable martial specialist whose strength comes from making sound tactical choices over the course of an adventure.

data.tagline: The detective who sees through every lie and finds every weakness.
data.overview: Inquisitive Rogues rely on sharp observation and insight instead of stealth alone. They specialize in uncovering hidden truths, exposing deception, and identifying openings that other adventurers might miss. This subclass shines for players who enjoy investigation, deduction, and carefully analyzing both mysteries and enemies before acting.

data.tagline: The right answer is often stronger than the strongest spell.
data.overview: Knowledge clerics thrive on preparation, investigation, and versatility outside combat while remaining capable support casters in battle. They gain extra skills and magical options that help uncover secrets, solve mysteries, and overcome obstacles through information rather than force. Playing one means asking, "What do I know that changes this situation?" just as often as, "Which spell should I cast?"

data.tagline: No one keeps a party standing longer than you do.
data.overview: Life clerics are the definitive healers of D&D, turning every healing spell into exceptional value while remaining capable combatants in their own right. Their greatest challenge is resisting the temptation to spend every turn healing. The strongest Life clerics know that preventing damage, defeating enemies, and healing at exactly the right moment will usually accomplish far more than trying to erase every injury as it happens.

data.tagline: Blind evil with holy radiance before it ever reaches your allies.
data.overview: Light clerics are among the most offensively focused clerics, wielding powerful fire and radiant spells to eliminate groups of enemies. They excel at deciding when to spend precious spell slots on explosive damage versus relying on the cleric's more traditional support tools. While they can still heal and protect like any cleric, they often prevent damage simply by ending fights quickly.

data.tagline: Sociable and stealthy.
data.overview:
As a lightfoot halfling, you can easily hide from notice, even using other people as cover. You're inclined to be affable and get along well with others. In the Forgotten Realms, lightfoot halflings have spread the farthest and thus are the most common variety.

Lightfoots are more prone to wanderlust than other halflings, and often dwell alongside other races or take up a nomadic life. In the world of Grayhawk, these halflings are called hairfeet or tallfellows.As a lightfoot halfling, you can easily hide from notice, even using other people as cover. You're inclined to be affable and get along well with others. In the Forgotten Realms, lightfoot halflings have spread the farthest and thus are the most common variety.

Lightfoots are more prone to wanderlust than other halflings, and often dwell alongside other races or take up a nomadic life. In the world of Grayhawk, these halflings are called hairfeet or tallfellows.

definingFeature.name: Naturally Stealthy
definingFeature.desc: Hide behind creatures at least one size larger than you.

data.tagline: Sorcerer of the three moons.
data.overview: Lunar sorcerers draw power from a chosen moon phase — Full, New, or Crescent — that shapes their bonus spells, Metamagic discounts, and lunar powers.

definingFeature.name: Lunar Embodiment
definingFeature.desc: Attune to a moon phase that colors your spells and abilities.

data.tagline: Win with strategy, manipulation, and making everyone else more effective.
data.overview: Masterminds excel at reading people, coordinating allies, and manipulating enemies through careful planning. While still capable of classic Rogue tactics, they lean toward support and social interaction, helping the entire party perform more effectively. Playing a Mastermind feels like directing the action from behind the scenes, always thinking several moves ahead.

data.tagline: A martial artist who weaponizes inner ki.
data.overview: Monks are agile martial artists who rely on speed, precision, and discipline instead of heavy armor or oversized weapons. Rather than overpowering enemies, they excel at controlling the pace of a fight—darting into vulnerable positions, delivering a flurry of strikes, then slipping away before retaliation. Many of their strongest techniques draw from a limited pool of inner energy, forcing them to decide when to push their offense, reinforce their defenses, or preserve resources for a more dangerous encounter. Although many monks share backgrounds rooted in rigorous training or spiritual practice, that discipline can manifest in countless ways, from secluded monastery traditions to street-fighting schools or wandering duelists.

definingFeature.name: Ki
definingFeature.desc: A pool of inner energy spent on Flurry of Blows, Patient Defense, Step of the Wind, and Stunning Strike — recharging on a short rest.

lore.role:
Mobile DPS / Skirmisher / Disruptor

Monks specialize in reaching high-priority enemies that other frontliners struggle to pressure. Rather than soaking damage, they weave through the battlefield, applying consistent melee pressure while using their mobility and control tools to isolate dangerous targets or escape unfavorable fights. As they gain levels, they also become unusually resilient against many status effects and ranged threats, making them difficult to pin down despite their lighter defenses.
lore.playstyle: Monk rewards careful positioning and resource management more than raw aggression. Nearly every turn asks whether spending precious Ki will create enough value now or whether it's better saved for a more dangerous fight later. They have answers for a wide variety of situations—mobility, defense, control, and offense—but rarely enough resources to excel at all of them simultaneously. Outside combat, their exceptional movement and physical abilities often let them solve traversal problems, infiltrate difficult locations, or accomplish athletic feats that few other classes can match. They're highly rewarding for players who enjoy constantly evaluating the battlefield, but can feel unforgiving when poor positioning or inefficient Ki usage leaves them exposed.
lore.subclasses: Every monk shares the same core identity: exceptional mobility, multiple attacks, and careful Ki management. Subclasses decide what that mobility is used for. Some focus almost entirely on overwhelming offense, while others become controllers, stealth specialists, defenders, or even limited spellcasters. Compared to many martial classes, monk subclasses often reshape the class's tactical priorities more than its overall rhythm—you'll still be managing Ki and moving aggressively around the battlefield, but the reason you're spending those resources can feel dramatically different.

data.tagline: Find the greatest threat—and make it your only target.
data.overview: Monster Slayer is built around identifying and dismantling dangerous individual enemies. Its features improve your ability to pressure priority targets, resist supernatural tricks, and disrupt creatures that rely on powerful magical abilities or unusual defenses. Rather than spreading attention across the battlefield, the subclass encourages disciplined target selection, rewarding players who can quickly recognize which enemy poses the greatest danger and commit their resources to bringing it down.

data.tagline: Strong and armored.
data.overview: As a mountain dwarf, you're strong and hardy, accustomed to a difficult life in rugged terrain. You're probably on the tall side (for a dwarf), and tend toward lighter coloration, The shield dwarves of northern Faerun, as well as the ruling Hylar clan and the noble Daewar clan of Dragonlance, are mountain dwarves.

definingFeature.name: Armor Training
definingFeature.desc: Proficiency with light and medium armor.

data.tagline: The wilderness is as much your ally as your faith.
data.overview: Nature clerics blend divine magic with the resilience and control associated with the natural world. They often solve encounters by slowing enemies, controlling movement, or resisting elemental threats rather than simply trading damage. Playing one rewards recognizing when controlling the battlefield will save more resources than trying to overpower it.

data.tagline: Control the battlefield through fear and relentless pressure.
data.overview: Conquest paladins are aggressive frontline controllers who excel at locking enemies down instead of simply defeating them. Many of their abilities frighten foes, reducing their mobility and forcing enemies to fight on your terms. If you enjoy dictating the pace of combat and slowly crushing your opponents rather than charging headfirst into battle, Conquest is an excellent choice.

data.tagline: The classic holy knight.
data.overview: Devotion is the most traditional paladin, emphasizing reliability, protection, and unwavering righteousness. Rather than specializing in a particular strategy, it strengthens nearly every aspect of the core paladin kit with defensive magic, improved weapon accuracy, and protection against evil influences. It's an approachable subclass that rewards solid fundamentals over complicated mechanics.

data.tagline: Lead from the front through athleticism and inspiration.
data.overview: Glory paladins focus on mobility, momentum, and empowering both themselves and nearby allies. They excel at reaching important targets, repositioning around the battlefield, and turning successful attacks into opportunities for the rest of the party. Compared to other paladins, Glory feels faster, more proactive, and less defensive.

data.tagline: Win battles without fighting whenever possible.
data.overview: Redemption encourages solving problems peacefully, but remains fully capable of defending allies when diplomacy fails. In combat, it focuses on protecting teammates, reducing incoming damage, and discouraging enemies from attacking your party. Rather than defeating enemies through overwhelming offense, Redemption succeeds by outlasting them and minimizing harm.

data.tagline: Hunt down priority targets and eliminate them.
data.overview: Vengeance trades some of the paladin's defensive focus for exceptional single-target offense. Its abilities help you pursue dangerous enemies, remain in melee, and deliver devastating damage to high-priority threats. If you enjoy playing the party's executioner—the one responsible for bringing down the biggest enemy on the field—Vengeance delivers that fantasy exceptionally well.

data.tagline: Become an unbreakable guardian against magical threats.
data.overview: Ancients specializes in resilience, particularly against spellcasters. Its abilities improve survivability for both the paladin and nearby allies while reinforcing the role of an enduring frontline protector. If your campaign features dangerous magic or you enjoy shielding your team from devastating spells, this oath shines.

data.tagline: Stand your ground and force enemies to deal with you.
data.overview: Crown leans furthest into the "tank" role by encouraging enemies to focus their attacks on you instead of your allies. Its abilities reward staying close to the party, intercepting damage, and holding the front line. Players who enjoy protecting teammates through positioning and battlefield presence will feel at home with this oath.

data.tagline: Protect the world from supernatural threats.
data.overview: Watchers specialize in combating extraplanar enemies such as fiends, aberrations, fey, and other magical creatures. Their abilities improve the party's resistance to magical influence while helping allies act more quickly in dangerous encounters. Although some features are campaign-dependent, they become exceptionally valuable in adventures filled with otherworldly foes.

data.tagline: Turn divine power toward domination instead of protection.
data.overview: The Oathbreaker abandons the ideals of a traditional paladin, replacing support-focused abilities with features that empower both themselves and, in some cases, undead allies. Rather than protecting companions, this subclass emphasizes personal power, fear, and commanding dark forces. While mechanically effective, it is generally intended for evil or morally corrupt characters and may not be appropriate for every campaign.

data.tagline: A disciplined team is stronger than any individual hero.
data.overview: Order clerics specialize in enabling allies to act more efficiently, often allowing teammates to capitalize immediately on the cleric's magic. Their turns are less about making the biggest play themselves and more about deciding which ally can make the best play if given the opportunity. They reward players who pay close attention to initiative order, positioning, and the strengths of the rest of the party.

data.tagline: The ultimate magical scholar whose spellbook is their greatest weapon.
data.overview: The Order of Scribes focuses on mastering magical knowledge itself, transforming the Wizard's spellbook into an active magical companion. Their abilities enhance spellcasting flexibility, allowing them to adapt spells and safely experiment with arcane power more easily than any other Wizard. Playing one feels like the consummate academic, solving problems through superior understanding rather than specializing in a single school of magic.

data.tagline: A holy warrior bound by a sacred oath.
data.overview:
Paladins are holy warriors who combine the martial prowess of a heavily armored knight with the divine magic of a devoted champion. Comfortable on the front lines, they wield powerful weapons while protecting allies with healing, defensive magic, and inspiring auras. Unlike fighters, who focus almost entirely on martial skill, paladins constantly balance their weapon attacks with a small but impactful selection of spells, making them one of the strongest hybrid classes in the game.

What truly defines a paladin, however, is their Oath. Their power comes from an unwavering commitment to a set of sacred ideals—whether that means justice, vengeance, devotion, redemption, or another calling. Those beliefs shape not only their abilities, but also how they approach difficult decisions throughout the campaign. A paladin's convictions are meant to be tested, making them one of the classes whose mechanics and roleplaying are most closely intertwined.

definingFeature.name: Divine Smite
definingFeature.desc: Expend a spell slot on a melee hit to deal a burst of radiant damage — nova potential that scales with the slot.

lore.role:
Tank / Bruiser / Support

Paladins thrive in the middle of the fight, protecting allies while posing a serious offensive threat themselves. They absorb punishment with heavy armor, bolster nearby teammates with defensive abilities, and can suddenly unleash devastating bursts of damage when an important enemy needs to fall. Their greatest strength is knowing which role is most valuable at any given moment: holding the line, supporting the party, or ending the fight with overwhelming force.
lore.playstyle: Paladins are easy to learn but reward good judgment. Their core gameplay is built around weapon attacks, making them approachable for new players, but every combat presents meaningful choices.
