Should you spend a spell slot on a powerful Divine Smite now, or save it for a more dangerous enemy? Should you heal a fallen ally, cast a support spell, or keep attacking to remove a threat before it acts again? While their toolkit is smaller than that of a full spellcaster, making the right decision at the right moment gives the class surprising depth.
lore.subclasses: Every paladin shares the same foundation of frontline combat, divine magic, and unwavering conviction, but each Sacred Oath defines what those ideals demand. Some emphasize protecting the innocent, others relentless justice, ancient traditions, conquest, or redemption. Your oath doesn't simply grant new abilities—it shapes how your character views the world, what they fight for, and sometimes what sacrifices they're willing to make. Even an Oathbreaker is defined by the oath they abandoned, making conviction the heart of every paladin's identity.

data.overview: Wild Magic Barbarians are infused with unpredictable magical energy that erupts whenever they enter battle. Each rage triggers a random magical effect, creating encounters that can play out differently every time while still preserving the class's powerful melee core. This subclass appeals to players who enjoy improvisation, adapting to unexpected advantages, and embracing a bit of controlled chaos without becoming a traditional spellcaster.
data.tagline: Every battle begins with a magical surprise that turns chaos into your greatest strength.
A spectral protector who shields allies by forcing enemies to face you instead.
data.overview: Ancestral Guardians call upon the spirits of their ancestors to defend those fighting beside them. Rather than simply enduring punishment, they actively discourage enemies from attacking their allies, making them one of the strongest defensive Barbarians in the game. Playing one feels like becoming the party's immovable protector, using supernatural guardians to control who your enemies can effectively threaten.

definingFeature.name: Ancestral Protectors
definingFeature.desc: Spirits hinder the foe you strike, shielding your allies from it.

data.tagline: Become the monster, growing natural weapons that adapt to every fight.
data.overview: The Beast Barbarian unleashes their primal instincts, transforming parts of their body into savage natural weapons. Claws, fangs, or a powerful tail each offer different advantages, encouraging players to adapt their approach depending on the situation. Playing one feels feral and unpredictable, blending brutal melee combat with surprising versatility and mobility.

data.tagline: The pure embodiment of reckless rage—more attacks, more aggression, no restraint.
data.overview: The Berserker embraces unrestrained fury, sacrificing caution in exchange for overwhelming aggression. This subclass focuses on staying in the middle of combat and attacking as often as possible, making it one of the most direct and offense-oriented Barbarians available. Playing a Berserker feels relentless—you push yourself beyond normal limits to keep the pressure on, thriving when fights end before your exhaustion catches up to you.

definingFeature.name: Frenzy
definingFeature.desc: Rage harder for a bonus-action attack every turn — at the cost of exhaustion when it ends.

data.tagline: Become the biggest threat on the battlefield—literally.
data.overview: Path of the Giant transforms the barbarian into a towering frontline presence that dominates space as much as it deals damage. Growing in size extends your reach, making it easier to lock down multiple enemies and threaten a much larger area, while elemental-infused attacks add consistent versatility to every swing. Unlike other barbarians that focus purely on surviving or hitting harder, the Giant specializes in controlling the battlefield through sheer physical presence—throwing creatures and objects, protecting allies by occupying critical space, and making it difficult for enemies to ignore you. It rewards players who enjoy feeling larger than life, using positioning and reach to shape the flow of combat rather than simply charging at the nearest target.

Storm Heralds channel the raw power of nature, cloaking themselves in an aura tied to the desert, sea, or tundra. Their abilities steadily influence the battlefield by damaging enemies, protecting allies, or wearing opponents down over time rather than relying on explosive bursts. This subclass suits players who enjoy creating constant pressure simply by staying in the heart of the fight.
data.tagline: Carry the fury of nature into battle, surrounding yourself with an ever-present elemental storm.
Channel the spirits of mighty beasts to become the Barbarian you want to be.
data.overview: The Totem Warrior forms a spiritual bond with powerful animal totems, each granting unique strengths that shape their role in combat. Whether emphasizing resilience, mobility, or battlefield utility, the subclass offers flexibility while keeping the Barbarian's durable front-line identity intact. It rewards players who enjoy tailoring their strengths to suit their preferred playstyle rather than committing to a single specialty.

The Zealot fights with unwavering divine conviction, blending brutal martial combat with holy power. They add reliable radiant or necrotic damage to their attacks and become exceptionally difficult to keep dead, making them relentless in both story and gameplay. The subclass shines in long adventuring days, where its resilience and consistent damage allow it to keep pushing forward when others begin to wear down.
data.tagline: A holy warrior whose faith makes them terrifyingly difficult to stop.
Keep the party together, and everyone becomes stronger.
data.overview: Peace clerics excel at strengthening allies before things go wrong rather than repairing the damage afterward. Much of their gameplay revolves around maintaining powerful bonds between teammates, deciding who benefits most from their support, and positioning themselves so those benefits reach the people who need them most. They often win encounters through consistency rather than dramatic moments.

data.tagline: Walk the line between life and death, carrying the whispers of departed souls.
data.overview: Phantoms draw supernatural power from the spirits of the dead, allowing them to spread their deadly strikes beyond a single target while gaining eerie knowledge from lingering souls. Their abilities make them feel mysterious and unsettling without abandoning the Rogue's core identity as a precise damage dealer. Playing a Phantom blends efficient combat with a haunting, otherworldly atmosphere that grows stronger as adventures become darker.

data.tagline: A disciplined soldier whose mind is as deadly as their blade.
data.overview: Psi Warriors augment their martial abilities with controlled psychic power, shielding allies, empowering attacks, and manipulating objects through pure mental force. Their abilities provide a balance of offense, defense, and utility without overwhelming the Fighter's straightforward combat style. Playing one feels versatile and composed, solving problems with both physical prowess and disciplined mental strength.

data.tagline: Their magic is best viewed as a flexible toolkit rather than a primary source of power.
data.overview: Rangers are adaptable martial specialists who combine skilled weapon combat with practical magic to stay effective in almost any situation. Rather than relying on overwhelming force or powerful spells alone, they excel by bringing the right tool to the right problem—whether that's tracking a dangerous quarry, scouting ahead, controlling the battlefield, or steadily wearing down priority targets. Their spellcasting is designed to enhance what they're already doing, providing mobility, utility, and tactical advantages instead of replacing their martial role. Just as valuable outside combat as within it, rangers are often the party members who spot danger first, navigate hostile environments, and help ensure the fight begins on favorable terms.

definingFeature.name: Favored Foe / Natural Explorer
definingFeature.desc: Mark prey for bonus damage and move through the wilds with supernatural ease — the hunter's edge.

lore.role:
Sustained DPS / Scout / Flex

Rangers deal reliable damage over the course of an encounter while adapting to changing situations with a versatile mix of weapons and utility spells. They can pressure key targets, support allies through battlefield control and exploration magic, and often contribute meaningful solutions outside of combat through scouting, survival, tracking, and problem-solving. Rather than dominating one phase of the game, rangers remain consistently useful across nearly every part of an adventure.
lore.playstyle: Ranger rewards preparation, positioning, and good decision-making over explosive turns. You'll often find yourself asking where you're needed most—whether that's eliminating a dangerous enemy, controlling space with a well-timed spell, scouting ahead, or solving a problem before combat even begins. Their magic is best viewed as a flexible toolkit rather than a primary source of power, giving them answers to a wide variety of situations without overshadowing their martial combat. Rangers are also one of the most self-sufficient classes, with the mobility, utility, and survivability to operate independently when necessary. They appeal to players who enjoy having meaningful options every encounter instead of repeating the same sequence of actions, but can feel underwhelming if played as a character who only attacks each turn or tries to compete with classes built around short bursts of overwhelming damage.
lore.subclasses: Few classes branch into as many distinct identities as the ranger. Some subclasses sharpen the ranger into an unmatched ambusher or monster hunter, while others introduce companions, powerful magical themes, or entirely new ways of controlling the battlefield. You might fight alongside an animal or draconic ally, step between planes, command supernatural swarms, embrace fey magic, or become a hunter who thrives in darkness. Despite these dramatically different flavors, every ranger remains rooted in the same foundation: a capable martial combatant whose versatility, preparation, and practical magic allow them to succeed in almost any environment or encounter.

data.tagline: Inventive tinkers.
data.overview: As a rock gnome, you have a natural inventiveness and hardiness beyond that of other gnomes. Most gnomes in the worlds of D&D are rock gnomes, including the tinker gnomes of the Dragonlance setting.

definingFeature.name: Tinker
definingFeature.desc: Construct tiny clockwork gadgets and gain expertise with magical lore.

data.tagline: A precise, elusive expert and skirmisher.
data.overview: Rogues are specialists who rely on precision, planning, and opportunity rather than brute force. They excel at exploiting weaknesses, striking when enemies are vulnerable, and solving problems through skill instead of raw power. Outside of combat, Rogues are among the most versatile adventurers in the game, often serving as scouts, infiltrators, detectives, diplomats, or experts at navigating dangerous environments. While every Rogue approaches challenges differently, they all reward clever thinking and making the most of every opportunity.

definingFeature.name: Sneak Attack
definingFeature.desc: Once per turn, add a burst of extra dice when you have advantage or an ally flanks — precision damage that scales steeply.

lore.role:
Single-Target DPS • Scout • Skill Specialist

Rogues focus on delivering high damage to individual targets rather than controlling large groups of enemies. Outside combat, they're often the party member best equipped to handle stealth, locks, traps, investigation, and other specialized tasks, making them invaluable during exploration and social encounters.
lore.playstyle: Playing a Rogue is about positioning and timing. Rather than standing toe-to-toe with enemies, you're constantly looking for openings, choosing high-value targets, and using mobility or cunning to stay one step ahead. The class has a moderate skill ceiling, rewarding players who think tactically both in and out of combat. Rogues also spend more time interacting with the world than many classes, often taking the lead during exploration, infiltration, and investigation.
lore.subclasses: Every Rogue shares the same core identity of precision, mobility, and expertise, but subclasses determine how they outsmart their opponents. Some embrace assassination or espionage, others weave magic into their tricks, while others become detectives, duelists, or even wield supernatural powers. No matter the path, every Rogue succeeds by creating advantages instead of overpowering enemies through force.
lore.understanding_sneak_attack_and_cunning_a:
Despite its name, Sneak Attack rarely requires you to be hidden. Most rogues trigger Sneak Attack simply by attacking an enemy that is distracted by one of your allies, allowing you to deal a large amount of extra damage once per turn. Rather than making several smaller attacks like a Fighter, the rogue is built around landing one accurate, high-impact strike every round. As you level up, Sneak Attack becomes one of the strongest sources of consistent single-target damage in the game, especially when combined with critical hits.

Supporting this playstyle is Cunning Action, which allows you to Dash, Disengage, or Hide as a Bonus Action. This gives rogues exceptional mobility, making it easy to weave in and out of combat, reposition for advantage, escape dangerous situations, or disappear from sight before striking again. Together, these two features define the rogue's combat identity: precise, mobile, and consistently effective.
lore.the_skill_monkey:
Outside of combat, the rogue is often considered the party's greatest skill user. While every character can attempt skill checks, rogues gain Expertise, allowing them to double their proficiency bonus in selected skills. Even at low levels, this can create bonuses that other characters simply cannot match. Whether you've specialized in Stealth, Persuasion, Deception, Investigation, Sleight of Hand, or Thieves' Tools, your character becomes remarkably reliable whenever those skills are called upon.

A rogue with Expertise isn't just slightly better than everyone else—they often become the obvious choice whenever that type of challenge appears. This allows rogues to influence adventures in ways that aren't measured by damage alone. Convincing a suspicious guard, tracking a hidden criminal, bypassing a complex lock, or sneaking an entire party through enemy territory can change the course of an adventure just as dramatically as winning a difficult battle.
lore.how_the_rogue_rewards_creativity:
The rogue is at its best when problems cannot be solved by force alone. While other classes often rely on powerful spells or combat abilities, rogues excel at finding opportunities where others see obstacles. In a bustling city, a rogue might uncover a criminal network by following the right person through the streets, forge documents that redirect suspicion onto a rival faction, or convince a guard captain that the party has official permission to be somewhere they absolutely should not be. When a noble's estate seems impossible to infiltrate, the rogue looks for servants, supply routes, forgotten entrances, and weaknesses in routine rather than stronger locks to pick.

Many of the rogue's most memorable victories happen before initiative is ever rolled. Their abilities encourage players to gather information, manipulate situations, exploit weaknesses, and create advantages that can completely change the direction of an adventure. While every class benefits from creative thinking, few classes reward it as consistently as the rogue. The more you pay attention to the people, places, and opportunities around you, the more powerful the class becomes. A skilled rogue doesn't just react to challenges—they often solve them before they become problems at all.

data.tagline: Harness the ancient magic of giants to become an unstoppable juggernaut.
data.overview: Rune Knights carve mystical runes that grant powerful passive benefits and unique activated abilities while channeling giant magic to grow to enormous size. Their toolkit blends durability, control, utility, and bursts of incredible strength, making them one of the most versatile Fighters available. Playing one feels imposing, combining ancient magical power with overwhelming physical presence.

definingFeature.name: Giant's Might
definingFeature.desc: Grow larger for advantage on Strength and bonus damage.

data.tagline: Calm discipline and unwavering resolve turned into overwhelming offense.
data.overview: The Samurai channels intense focus and determination to perform at their absolute best when the pressure is highest. Their abilities emphasize accuracy, resilience, and moments of explosive offensive power while also granting unexpected strengths in social situations through force of personality. Playing one feels composed and decisive, waiting for the perfect moment before unleashing a devastating assault.

data.tagline: The magical guardian who turns defensive spells into powerful protection.
data.overview: Abjurers specialize in wards, barriers, and protective magic that keep themselves and their allies safe from harm. Rather than simply avoiding danger, they actively reduce or negate it, becoming remarkably durable despite their fragile reputation. Playing an Abjurer feels reassuring, rewarding players who enjoy protecting the party while remaining an exceptionally capable spellcaster.

Conjurers excel at bringing creatures, objects, and magical effects into existence, making them one of the most versatile Wizard traditions. Whether creating useful tools, teleporting across the battlefield, or filling the fight with summoned allies, they constantly expand the party's options. Playing one feels inventive, solving problems by making exactly what the situation demands appear out of thin air.
data.tagline: Summon creatures, objects, and allies wherever they're needed most.
Knowledge is power—and you already know what's about to happen.
data.overview: Diviners peer into possible futures, gaining extraordinary insight into both the world and the outcome of important events. Their signature abilities allow them to manipulate key rolls, making them one of the most consistently influential Wizards regardless of the situation. Playing one feels almost prophetic, rewarding careful timing and the confidence that comes from seeing what others cannot.

data.tagline: Why fight fairly when you can simply change someone's mind?
data.overview: Enchanters specialize in influencing thoughts, emotions, and behavior through subtle but powerful magic. Rather than defeating enemies through force alone, they charm, disable, or redirect opponents to control encounters with minimal violence. Playing one feels clever and manipulative, thriving whenever words or magical persuasion can accomplish more than destruction.

data.tagline: The classic battle mage, hurling devastating spells with pinpoint precision.
data.overview: Evokers master destructive elemental magic, unleashing explosive spells while learning to protect allies from the worst of their own attacks. They remain one of the strongest choices for players who enjoy direct magical offense without sacrificing the Wizard's overall versatility. Playing one feels spectacular, filling the battlefield with fire, lightning, ice, and raw arcane power.

data.tagline: Reality is optional if your enemies believe the illusion.
data.overview: Illusionists deceive the senses with convincing magical creations that confuse, distract, and outwit their opponents. Their effectiveness depends as much on creativity as raw mechanics, rewarding players who enjoy thinking beyond straightforward spellcasting. Playing one feels imaginative and unpredictable, often overcoming challenges through deception instead of direct confrontation.

data.tagline: Command the forces of life and death to build an undead army.
data.overview: Necromancers explore the magic of death, life force, and undeath, draining vitality from enemies while empowering themselves and raising undead servants. Although famous for commanding skeletons and zombies, they also excel at enduring difficult fights through careful manipulation of life energy. Playing one feels methodical, gradually building momentum as your dark magic and loyal minions overwhelm your enemies.

data.tagline: Reshape the world by changing the nature of people and objects.
data.overview: Transmuters specialize in altering the physical properties of creatures, materials, and even themselves. Their magic provides exceptional versatility, enhancing allies, overcoming obstacles, and adapting to changing situations through transformation rather than destruction. Playing one feels resourceful, constantly asking, "What if I simply changed this into something more useful?"

data.tagline: The wilderness expert who strikes fast and never gets pinned down.
data.overview: Scouts combine the Rogue's precision with the survival instincts of an experienced explorer. Exceptional mobility and wilderness expertise allow them to control distance, reposition constantly, and guide the party safely through dangerous terrain. Playing one feels like a hunter who always knows where to stand and when to move.

data.tagline: Strike from darkness and refuse to stay dead when others would fall.
data.overview: Shadow Magic embraces stealth, survival, and the unsettling powers of darkness. It gains tools that make the sorcerer unusually difficult to kill while enhancing mobility, vision, and battlefield presence in dim environments. Rather than overwhelming enemies with raw force, it thrives through persistence, positioning, and an aura of supernatural menace.

definingFeature.name: Hound of Ill Omen
definingFeature.desc: Spend sorcery points to summon a shadow hound that hunts one foe.

data.tagline: A born spellcaster who bends magic itself.
data.overview: Sorcerers are spellcasters whose magic comes from within rather than from study, faith, or bargains with powerful beings. Their power is an innate part of who they are, often tied to an extraordinary bloodline, a magical event, or a supernatural transformation. While they know fewer spells than many other casters, they possess an unparalleled ability to shape, enhance, and adapt those spells in the moment. A sorcerer isn't defined by having the right spell for every situation—they're defined by making the spells they do know exceptionally effective.

definingFeature.name: Metamagic
definingFeature.desc: Spend sorcery points to twin, quicken, subtle, or empower spells — customizing each cast on the fly.

lore.role:
Primary Role: DPS / Controller / Flex

Sorcerers specialize in delivering explosive magical offense while adapting their spells to fit the situation. They can devastate groups of enemies, focus down dangerous single targets, or reshape the battlefield with disabling effects. Depending on their origin, they may also branch into support, durability, or utility, but magical offense remains their strongest identity.
lore.playstyle: Sorcerers are all about efficiency and amplification. Their unique resource allows them to modify spells in powerful ways—casting faster, farther, more subtly, or affecting multiple targets where others cannot. This makes them one of the most explosive spellcasters in the game, capable of producing turns that dramatically shift the course of combat. Outside battle, they rely more on creativity than breadth, since they know relatively few spells, but their natural charisma often makes them excellent negotiators, performers, or leaders. The class is moderately complex, rewarding players who understand when to spend resources for maximum impact.
lore.subclasses: Every sorcerer shares the same core identity: innate magical power refined through mastery of their own abilities rather than an external source. The subclasses define where that power originates and how it expresses itself. Some origins make the sorcerer tougher, others emphasize chaos, psychic abilities, divine blessings, battlefield control, or overwhelming elemental force. While all sorcerers bend magic to their will, each subclass changes the flavor—and often the role—of that magical talent.

data.tagline: A silent psychic assassin whose greatest weapon exists only in the mind.
data.overview: Soulknives wield blades forged from pure psychic energy, allowing them to fight without conventional weapons while enhancing their skills through mental power. Their abilities make them exceptionally reliable, adaptable, and difficult to disarm or anticipate, whether sneaking through enemy territory or confronting foes directly. Playing a Soulknife feels smooth and self-sufficient, with psychic abilities quietly improving nearly everything the Rogue already does well.

data.tagline: Ride the winds into battle and let lightning answer your every move.
data.overview: Storm Sorcery combines elemental magic with constant mobility, encouraging the sorcerer to reposition freely while unleashing thunder and lightning. It excels at weaving through combat without becoming trapped, making movement a key part of its offense and defense. The subclass feels fast, dynamic, and energetic, rewarding players who are always looking for the next advantageous position.

definingFeature.name: Heart of the Storm
definingFeature.desc: Casting storm spells erupts in lightning or thunder around you.

data.tagline: Tough and poison-resistant.
data.overview: As a stout halfling, you're hardier than average and have some resistance to poison. Some say that stouts have dwarven blood. In the Forgotten Realms, these halflings are called stronghearts, and they're most common in the south.

definingFeature.name: Stout Resilience
definingFeature.desc: Advantage on saves against poison and resistance to poison damage.

data.tagline: Command the battlefield through a thousand tiny allies.
data.overview: Swarmkeeper surrounds the ranger with a supernatural swarm that transforms nearly every successful attack into an opportunity to reposition creatures, move yourself, or add extra damage. Rather than simply increasing offensive power, the swarm constantly creates small tactical advantages that accumulate over the course of a fight. Players who enjoy evaluating multiple good options each turn will appreciate the flexibility, as deciding whether to push enemies, reposition yourself, or maximize damage often depends on the evolving state of the battlefield rather than a fixed rotation.

Swashbucklers trade skulking in the shadows for confidence and flair, thriving in fast-paced melee duels. Their mobility and charisma allow them to dart through combat, pressure isolated enemies, and escape retaliation with ease. Playing one feels energetic and cinematic, rewarding bold plays and constant movement rather than careful hiding.
data.tagline: A daring duelist who turns every fight into a stylish one-on-one contest.
Curious, catlike wanderers and collectors.
data.overview:
Created by the Cat Lord—a divine being of the Upper Planes—to blend the qualities of humanoids and cats, tabaxi are a varied people in both attitude and appearance. In some lands, tabaxi live like the cats they resemble, naturally curious and at home in playful environments. In other places, tabaxi live as other folk do, not exhibiting the feline behavior the Cat Lord intended.

Tabaxi’s appearance is as varied as their attitudes. Some tabaxi have features or patterning in their fur like tigers, jaguars, or other big cats, while others have appearances more like a house cat. Still others have unique patterns or might style their fur to their preferences — or might even be hairless!

definingFeature.name: Cat's Talent & Feline Agility
definingFeature.desc: Natural climbers and stealthy hunters who can burst to double speed in a sprint.

lore.society: Restless travelers who collect tales and curios, rarely staying anywhere long.
lore.physical: Lithe and cat-like, with fur, retractable claws, and a long tail.

data.tagline: Meet every attack with thunder, lightning, and overwhelming force.
data.overview: Tempest clerics are aggressive frontline casters who combine heavy armor with devastating elemental magic. Their abilities reward staying close enough to punish enemies that engage them while still looking for opportunities to unleash powerful lightning or thunder spells on clustered foes. The constant decision is whether to hold the line beside your allies or spend your turn delivering a decisive magical strike.

data.tagline: Dance around danger, confuse your enemies, and let chaos win the battle for you.
data.overview: The Archfey patron transforms the warlock into a master of enchantment, illusion, and mobility. Rather than overwhelming enemies with raw force, it specializes in charming, frightening, and repositioning foes while slipping safely out of danger. An Archfey warlock often feels elusive, winning encounters by making opponents waste actions or fight on unfavorable terms instead of simply dealing more damage.

definingFeature.name: Fey Presence
definingFeature.desc: Charm or frighten creatures around you with a burst of fey power.

data.tagline: Bring divine light to a class better known for darkness.
data.overview: The Celestial patron blends the warlock's offensive toolkit with meaningful healing and support. While still capable of dealing respectable damage, it can rescue allies, restore health without sacrificing its limited spellcasting, and provide valuable utility that most warlocks cannot. It feels like a hybrid between a damage dealer and an emergency support caster, making it one of the class's most team-oriented options.

definingFeature.name: Healing Light
definingFeature.desc: Spend a pool of d6s as a bonus action to heal creatures at range.

data.tagline: Control the battlefield like the tide itself, dragging enemies exactly where you want them.
data.overview: The Fathomless specializes in battlefield control through summoned tentacles, movement reduction, and defensive utility. It steadily limits enemy mobility while protecting itself and its allies from incoming threats, making encounters feel increasingly one-sided as the battlefield bends to its will. Rather than explosive bursts of power, it excels through persistent pressure and positional control.

definingFeature.name: Tentacle of the Deeps
definingFeature.desc: Summon a spectral tentacle to lash foes for cold damage and slow them.

data.tagline: Turn every victory into momentum and burn through anything standing in your way.
data.overview: The Fiend is the classic offensive warlock, rewarding aggression with durability and destructive magic. It gains temporary resilience whenever enemies fall, allowing it to stay in the fight while unleashing devastating firepower and explosive area attacks. Among warlocks, it leans furthest toward straightforward magical destruction while still retaining the class's signature versatility.

definingFeature.name: Dark One's Blessing
definingFeature.desc: Gain temporary hit points each time you drop a hostile creature.

data.tagline: Your patron grants incredible versatility—and a place to call home almost anywhere.
data.overview: The Genie offers one of the most adaptable warlock experiences, adding elemental power, exceptional utility, and unique exploration tools. Depending on the chosen genie, the subclass gains different magical flavors while sharing a strong focus on mobility, sustained damage, and creative problem solving. Its signature magical refuge also gives it unmatched flexibility during travel, infiltration, and downtime.

definingFeature.name: Genie's Vessel
definingFeature.desc: A magical vessel grants an extradimensional refuge and bonus elemental damage.

data.tagline: Win battles by breaking minds instead of bodies.
data.overview: The Great Old One embraces psychic manipulation, subtle influence, and unsettling mental abilities. It focuses less on direct damage and more on information gathering, communication, fear, and disrupting enemy decision-making. Playing one often feels like staying several steps ahead, turning knowledge and psychological pressure into powerful advantages.

definingFeature.name: Entropic Ward
definingFeature.desc: Turn an enemy's attack into disadvantage — and a miss into your own advantage.

data.tagline: Turn forbidden magic into deadly martial skill.
data.overview: The Hexblade reshapes the warlock into a formidable weapon user without abandoning its magical identity. It combines powerful melee combat with curses, defensive magic, and supernatural precision, creating a character who can comfortably fight on the front lines while still casting potent spells. The subclass rewards balancing swordplay and spellcasting rather than committing entirely to either.

definingFeature.name: Hexblade's Curse
definingFeature.desc: Mark a foe for bonus damage, easier crits, and a heal when it dies.

data.tagline: Become the monster your enemies fear before they ever touch you.
data.overview: The Undead patron revolves around fear, resilience, and gradually transforming into an avatar of death itself. It excels at frightening enemies, resisting retaliation, and surviving encounters that would overwhelm more fragile spellcasters. Playing an Undead warlock feels increasingly oppressive over the course of a fight, as opponents struggle to stand their ground against a relentless supernatural presence.

definingFeature.name: Form of Dread
definingFeature.desc: Transform into a terrifying aspect for temp HP and fear on your strikes.

data.tagline: Pact with one who cheated death.
data.overview: Undying warlocks borrow the resilience of a deathless lord — warding off undead and clawing back life whenever death draws near.

definingFeature.name: Defy Death
definingFeature.desc: Heal yourself whenever you cheat death or pull someone else back.

data.tagline: The classic adventurer—fast hands, quick feet, and always one step ahead.
data.overview: The Thief doubles down on the Rogue's natural agility and talent for infiltration, becoming exceptionally skilled at climbing, sneaking, and making use of objects in the middle of combat. Rather than relying on flashy abilities, it rewards creative problem-solving and clever use of the environment. Playing a Thief feels flexible and resourceful, turning every battlefield and dungeon into a playground of opportunities.

data.tagline: Fiend-blooded folk marked by infernal heritage.
data.overview: To be greeted with stares and whispers, to suffer violence and insult on the street, to see mistrust and fear in every eye: this is the lot of the tiefling. And to twist the knife, tieflings know that this is because a pact struck generations ago infused the essence of Asmodeus, overlord of the Nine Hells (and many of the other powerful devils serving under him) into their bloodline. Their appearance and their nature are not their fault but the result of an ancient sin, for which they and their children and their children's children will always be held accountable.

definingFeature.name: Infernal Legacy
definingFeature.desc: Resistance to fire and a bloodline of innate spells (a cantrip plus level-gated spells).

lore.society: Often distrusted or shunned for their heritage, many become self-reliant and wary, forging found families. Bloodlines tie them to specific archdevils.
lore.physical: Human-like but unmistakably fiendish — horns, a tail, unusual skin tones, and solid-colored eyes.

data.overview: Trickery clerics rely on deception, illusion, stealth, and misdirection to give their party the upper hand. Their strongest turns often come from creating confusion, improving an ally's positioning, or avoiding a fight altogether rather than dealing direct damage. Success comes from recognizing opportunities that more straightforward classes would never even consider.
data.tagline: Victory doesn't always come from fighting fair.
Stand between darkness and your companions, making every ally harder to bring down.
data.overview: Twilight clerics are exceptional protectors, surrounding nearby allies with persistent defensive magic while remaining surprisingly capable combatants themselves. They constantly weigh whether to stay near the party to maximize their protective abilities or move into a better position for spellcasting or offense. Few subclasses are better at helping an entire group survive long, difficult encounters.

data.tagline: Driven heirs to an eternal tradition of competition.
data.overview:
The Uma are a proud people whose isolated clans dwell in distant, often fey-touched planes, far removed from the concerns of most civilizations. Every aspect of Uma life revolves around the pursuit of physical excellence. Strength, speed, endurance, precision, and discipline are not merely admired—they are the highest expressions of virtue. From childhood onward, every Uma trains relentlessly, refining their chosen specialty in preparation for the great festivals where competitors test themselves in contests ranging from footraces and wrestling to elaborate displays of athletic ingenuity. Victory earns not wealth, but honor: champions are granted lifelong titles and immortal remembrance among their people.
---
Despite their competitive nature, Uma society is built upon profound mutual respect. Fair competition is sacred, and the rules of any contest are upheld with almost religious devotion. Clever tactics, superior preparation, and creative use of one's surroundings are celebrated as marks of mastery, but deception outside the agreed bounds of a contest is considered deeply dishonorable. This philosophy extends into daily life, producing individuals who are disciplined, direct, and surprisingly gracious in both victory and defeat.
---
Ancestor reverence shapes nearly every Uma tradition. Great competitors are remembered through carved monuments, statues, and memorials that preserve their achievements for generations to come. Artistic expression exists almost exclusively to commemorate excellence, ensuring that every legendary athlete becomes part of the clan's living history. Uma names often resemble those of celebrated racehorses—grand, evocative, or simply memorable combinations of words such as Summer Anthem, King Cobalt, or Resonant Whisper. Some names even include apparent titles, such as Emperor Vardus or Admiral, though these carry no authority of their own. True titles can only be earned through victory in competition, often leading to amusing misunderstandings when outsiders mistake a name for a rank.
---
While most Uma spend their entire lives within their clan, many eventually depart on a period of adventure. Such journeys are viewed as an advanced stage of training rather than exile or wanderlust. By testing themselves against unfamiliar lands, monsters, and cultures, these travelers seek experiences impossible to find within the safety of their homeland before returning to compete with newfound skill and perspective.

definingFeature.name: Natural Affinity
definingFeature.desc: A fey bond grants proficiency in a nature-themed skill of your choice.

lore.lifespan: Uma mature at roughly the same rate as humans and typically live between 80 and 100 years. Their lives are often divided into distinct stages: years of disciplined training, a prime spent earning honors through competition, and a respected elderhood dedicated to teaching younger generations and preserving the legacy of the clan's greatest champions.
lore.society: Uma clans are close-knit communities where personal achievement strengthens the honor of the entire people. Seasonal festivals serve as both celebration and proving ground, with competitors earning titles that become part of their identity for the rest of their lives. Even outside formal competition, Uma naturally seek opportunities to improve themselves, viewing hardship and adventure alike as valuable training rather than misfortune.
lore.physical: Uma are tall, athletic humanoids with distinctly equine features, including horse-like ears, expressive tails, and powerful lower bodies built for explosive speed and endurance. Their physiques are lean but muscular, reflecting lifetimes spent training for feats of strength and agility.
lore.alignment: Most Uma lean toward lawful alignments, believing that clear rules are essential for fair competition and honorable conduct. They value integrity above kindness or cruelty, respecting worthy opponents regardless of their intentions. While they readily accept cunning tactics employed within an agreed contest, they have little tolerance for lies, broken promises, or victories won through betrayal rather than merit.

data.tagline: Fight beside your allies instead of behind them.
data.overview: War clerics blur the line between warrior and spellcaster, comfortably mixing weapon attacks with divine magic throughout a fight. They frequently choose between spending a turn making another attack or casting one of the cleric's many impactful spells, making action economy one of the subclass's defining challenges. The best War clerics know when steel is enough—and when only divine intervention will turn the battle.

data.tagline: A battlefield mage who balances devastating offense with disciplined defense.
data.overview: War Mages are trained for combat above all else, combining powerful spellcasting with enhanced defenses that help them survive dangerous encounters. Rather than focusing entirely on one school of magic, they emphasize reliability, maintaining concentration, and staying effective even under heavy pressure. Playing one feels confident and resilient, standing firm where many other Wizards would be forced to retreat.

definingFeature.name: Arcane Deflection
definingFeature.desc: React to boost your AC or a save, at the cost of casting only cantrips next turn.

data.tagline: A pact-bound wielder of borrowed power.
data.overview: Warlocks are spellcasters who draw their power from a supernatural patron rather than years of study or innate magical talent. Their abilities revolve around a bargain with an immensely powerful being—whether that patron is benevolent, manipulative, or something far stranger. Unlike other full casters, warlocks wield a relatively small number of spells but cast them at consistently high power, supplementing them with customizable magical talents and one of the strongest repeatable magical attacks in the game. The class thrives on making deliberate choices that shape its identity, allowing two warlocks with the same patron to feel mechanically distinct.

definingFeature.name: Pact Magic
definingFeature.desc: A handful of always-max-level spell slots that recharge on a short rest, backed by invocations and a pact boon.

lore.role:
Primary Role: Flexible DPS / Controller / Utility

Warlocks fill the role of a reliable ranged damage dealer that can shift into powerful support or control whenever the moment demands it. Unlike other spellcasters, a warlock isn't expected to solve every encounter with a different spell. Instead, you'll spend most combats contributing steady damage through Eldritch Blast while waiting for the right opportunity to spend one of your limited spell slots. This consistency makes the warlock one of the most dependable damage dealers in the game, able to contribute meaningful damage every round without worrying about running out of resources. When the situation truly calls for it, however, your spells can completely change the course of a fight, making every spell slot feel impactful rather than routine.
lore.playstyle: The warlock rewards patience, planning, and specialization over adaptability. Combat itself is often straightforward, but deciding when to commit your limited resources is one of the class's greatest challenges. Spending a spell too early can leave you without an answer later, while holding onto it for too long may mean you never use it at all. Outside of combat, your high Charisma often places you in the role of the party's spokesperson, making social encounters just as important as battles. While the warlock isn't mechanically difficult to pilot from round to round, it asks you to make meaningful decisions both in character creation and throughout an adventuring day. Success comes less from mastering complicated turns and more from understanding your strengths, recognizing the moments where your magic matters most, and knowing when consistency is more valuable than spectacle.
lore.subclasses: Every warlock shares the same foundation of consistent ranged combat, powerful but limited spellcasting, and deep customization through Invocations and Pact Boons. Your Patron doesn't replace that identity—it shapes how you apply it. Some Patrons encourage battlefield control, others improve your survivability, enhance your spellcasting, or introduce entirely new tactical options. Rather than dramatically changing your role within the party, your subclass refines the type of warlock you become, allowing two warlocks to feel remarkably different while still sharing the same core philosophy of making a few powerful choices exceptionally well.
lore.your_magic_isn_t_your_main_weapon: New players often assume a warlock fights like a wizard, constantly casting powerful spells throughout the day. In reality, your primary weapon is usually Eldritch Blast. Think of it less as a backup cantrip and more as the magical equivalent of a fighter's sword or a ranger's bow—it's what you'll rely on in nearly every combat. Your spell slots aren't there to replace your consistent damage; they're there to create defining moments. Once you embrace that mindset, the class begins to feel far more natural, and every spell you cast carries a sense of importance that many other spellcasters rarely experience.
lore.understanding_your_spell_slots: The warlock's spellcasting is unlike any other class in D&D, and understanding it is essential to enjoying the class. You have very few spell slots, but they are always cast at your highest available level and are recovered after a Short Rest rather than a Long Rest. This creates a unique rhythm where your power can fluctuate depending on how your group approaches an adventuring day. In campaigns with regular Short Rests, warlocks can cast powerful spells surprisingly often. In campaigns where rests are rare, however, you'll need to be much more conservative with your magic. Before choosing a warlock, it's worth understanding how your group and Dungeon Master typically pace their adventures, as it can have a noticeable impact on how the class feels to play.
lore.your_depth_comes_from_your_choices: Although the warlock's turns in combat are often simple, the class offers some of the deepest character customization in the game. Invocations, Pact Boons, spell selection, feats, and your Patron all combine to shape what your character is capable of both in and out of combat. Many of these choices are permanent or difficult to change, making each level-up feel significant. Rather than giving you an answer to every problem, the warlock encourages you to become exceptionally good at a handful of things. The class rewards players who enjoy carefully crafting a unique build, finding creative uses for specialized abilities, and making every decision count.

data.tagline: Fluid and amphibious.
data.overview:
Water genasi descend from marids, aquatic genies from the Elemental Plane of Water. Water genasi are perfectly suited to life underwater and carry the power of the waves inside themselves.

Their skin is often shades of blue or green, sometimes a blend of the two. If they have a human skin tone, there is a glistening texture that catches the light, like water droplets or nearly invisible fish scales. Their hair can resemble seaweed, waving as if in a current, or it can even be like water itself.

definingFeature.name: Call to the Wave
definingFeature.desc: A swim speed, amphibious breathing, acid resistance, and Shape Water.

data.tagline: Strike with one hand, restore with the other.
data.overview: Way of Mercy turns the monk into one of the game's most flexible combat medics without sacrificing its martial identity. Rather than dedicating turns exclusively to healing, Mercy weaves restoration and debilitating strikes directly into its normal attack routine, allowing it to stabilize allies or punish enemies without abandoning the front line. Its signature abilities reward good target selection and efficient Ki usage, making it excellent at adapting to changing fights. Compared to other monks, Mercy is the least selfish—its mobility isn't just for reaching enemies, but also for reaching teammates who suddenly need help.

data.tagline: Win the fight before the enemy knows where you are.
data.overview: Way of Shadow leans heavily into infiltration, stealth, and battlefield positioning. It expands the monk's already exceptional mobility with tools that let it bypass obstacles, disappear into darkness, and approach vulnerable targets from unexpected angles. While its direct damage isn't dramatically higher than other monks, it spends far less time trying to force fights through defended front lines. Outside combat, Shadow gains some of the strongest scouting and infiltration tools available to any martial class, making it especially rewarding in campaigns where information and positioning matter as much as combat itself.

data.tagline: Fight with spirit instead of muscle.
data.overview: Astral Self summons spectral arms and eventually an entire spiritual manifestation that augments the monk's combat abilities. Rather than simply making the monk stronger, these manifestations reshape how it engages enemies by extending its reach, improving reliability, and allowing different ability scores to support its attacks. The subclass feels steadier than many monks, relying less on perfect positioning and more on maintaining an empowered combat form throughout important encounters. It's ideal for players who enjoy building momentum as a fight progresses instead of constantly darting in and out.

data.tagline: Turn every missed attack into another opening.
data.overview: Despite its name, Drunken Master isn't about drinking—it's about unpredictable movement and impossible-to-follow footwork. This subclass excels at weaving through crowded battlefields, slipping away from enemies after attacking, and positioning itself where retaliation is difficult. As fights become more chaotic, Drunken Master often becomes harder to pin down, flowing between opponents while avoiding being surrounded. It rewards players who constantly think several movements ahead and enjoy making enemy positioning work against them.

data.tagline: Trade relentless punches for a wider toolbox.
data.overview: Way of the Four Elements gives the monk access to elemental techniques inspired by traditional spellcasters, allowing it to project force, manipulate the battlefield, and solve problems that pure martial characters often cannot. Rather than specializing further in mobility or melee pressure, it broadens the monk's options with ranged attacks and utility abilities fueled by the same Ki resource. This flexibility comes at a cost, however—balancing elemental techniques against the monk's core abilities creates one of the most resource-intensive playstyles in the class. Players who enjoy adapting to unusual situations may appreciate its versatility, but it demands careful planning to avoid running out of fuel too early.

data.tagline: Perfect the weapon until it becomes an extension of yourself.
data.overview: Kensei shifts the monk's focus from unarmed combat toward mastery of carefully chosen weapons. Rather than abandoning the monk's mobility, it combines precise weapon strikes with agile defensive techniques, creating a fighter that constantly alternates between offense and protection. The subclass is mechanically straightforward compared to many monks, emphasizing consistent performance over explosive turns. It appeals to players who like the monk's speed but prefer fighting with blades, bows, or other traditional weapons instead of relying almost entirely on their fists.

data.tagline: Master the fundamentals until every strike controls the fight.
data.overview: Open Hand doubles down on the monk's original strengths instead of introducing an entirely new gimmick. Its attacks disrupt enemy positioning, knock foes off balance, deny reactions, and generally make every successful hit more impactful than simple damage alone. Rather than chasing flashy mechanics, it reinforces the monk's role as a battlefield controller who dictates where enemies can safely stand and how effectively they can respond. If the base monk represents the class in its purest form, Open Hand is that philosophy refined to its highest level.

data.tagline: Bring the monk's speed to every range.
data.overview: Sun Soul allows the monk to project radiant energy instead of relying solely on melee attacks, giving it a reliable ranged option without abandoning its martial rhythm. Rather than transforming into a dedicated spellcaster, it remains a monk first, using mobility to constantly adjust distance while peppering enemies with radiant strikes. The subclass is especially comfortable against flying or hard-to-reach opponents that frustrate most melee characters, though it generally trades some peak melee efficiency for this added flexibility. Players who enjoy staying active around the edges of combat while always threatening meaningful damage will find its toolkit especially appealing.

data.tagline: Embrace unpredictability and let chaos decide what happens next.
data.overview: Wild Magic turns every spell into a gamble, with unpredictable magical surges capable of helping or hindering anyone nearby. While experienced players can influence that randomness to some extent, chance remains an ever-present companion. Playing a Wild Magic sorcerer is less about perfect planning and more about adapting to unexpected opportunities—and surviving the occasional disaster.

definingFeature.name: Wild Magic Surge
definingFeature.desc: Your spells can trigger a random effect from the d100 surge table.

data.tagline: The scholar who masters all arcane magic.
data.overview: Wizards are the ultimate students of magic, wielding the broadest and most versatile spellcasting arsenal in the game. Unlike other spellcasters who draw power from faith, bloodlines, or natural talent, Wizards earn their abilities through years of study, experimentation, and mastery of arcane knowledge. Given enough preparation, they can adapt to nearly any challenge, solving problems with carefully chosen spells instead of brute force. Regardless of specialization, every Wizard rewards planning, creativity, and an understanding that knowledge is often the greatest source of power.

definingFeature.name: Spellbook & Arcane Recovery
definingFeature.desc: Collect spells in a book and prepare a fresh arsenal each day, recovering slots through study — the deepest toolbox of any class.

lore.role:
Controller • Utility • AoE DPS • Problem Solver

Wizards shape encounters more than almost any other class. They can devastate groups of enemies, lock down powerful threats, solve problems no other class can, and adapt to almost any situation through careful spell selection. While they can deal tremendous damage, their greatest strength is recognizing what a situation demands and having the right spell to answer it.
lore.playstyle:
Playing a Wizard is about preparation, judgment, and understanding your role within the party. Unlike many classes that solve problems with a consistent set of abilities, Wizards succeed by choosing from an enormous library of spells, weighing which option will have the greatest impact, and deciding whether a problem is worth spending precious resources to solve.

Combat is rarely about casting the biggest spell available. Every turn presents meaningful decisions: eliminate weaker enemies, disable the strongest threat, protect an ally, counter an enemy spellcaster, conserve spell slots, or reshape the battlefield entirely. Learning when each approach is most effective is what separates an experienced Wizard from a novice.

Outside combat, Wizards continue to contribute through ritual magic, utility spells, research, and magical knowledge. Their spellbook allows them to prepare for challenges before they happen, often solving obstacles that would otherwise require entirely different approaches.
lore.subclasses: Every Wizard shares the same core identity: a scholar whose growing spellbook offers unmatched flexibility and answers to almost any challenge. Subclasses don't replace this identity—they simply determine the areas of magic the Wizard becomes especially proficient in. Some become exceptional battlefield controllers, others specialize in destructive magic, protection, illusion, summoning, or manipulating time, gravity, and fate. Regardless of specialization, every Wizard remains defined by careful preparation, intelligent decision-making, and an ever-expanding arsenal of spells.
lore.is_wizard_right_for_you:
The Wizard is often considered one of the highest skill ceiling classes in D&D. While it's perfectly possible to enjoy the class with a handful of reliable spells, mastering it means understanding hundreds of spell options, planning ahead for future challenges, and recognizing which spell will have the greatest impact in any given situation.

Players who enjoy learning systems, planning ahead, and making tactical decisions every turn will often find Wizard one of the most rewarding classes in the game. Those looking for a straightforward character that performs consistently without much preparation may find other spellcasters—or martial classes—more approachable.
lore.the_wizard_s_responsibility:
Unlike many classes, a Wizard rarely succeeds on their own.

With limited defenses and relatively low durability, Wizards depend heavily on their party to create space, protect their concentration, and keep dangerous enemies away. In return, the Wizard is expected to solve problems that few other characters can.

Your allies may hold the front line while you eliminate an entire wave of enemies with a single spell. They may protect you while you maintain a spell that completely controls the battlefield. They may rely on you to teleport the party to safety, dispel dangerous magic, identify ancient artifacts, or counter an enemy spellcaster at exactly the right moment.

This creates a unique relationship between the Wizard and the rest of the party. Your teammates help keep you alive because they trust you to make the decisions that can completely change the outcome of an encounter.
lore.the_spellbook:
A Wizard's spellbook is more than a class feature—it's the heart of the class.

Unlike most spellcasters, Wizards can continually expand their collection of spells by copying magic from scrolls, spellbooks, and other sources discovered throughout a campaign. Every new spell permanently increases the number of tools available to you, allowing your character to grow not just through leveling up, but through exploration and discovery.

Building a spellbook means making long-term decisions about the kinds of problems you want to solve. No Wizard can prepare every spell at once, so choosing what to bring each day becomes part of the class's strategic depth.

data.tagline: Swift and stealthy.
data.overview:
As a wood elf, you have keen senses and intuition, and your fleet feet carry you quickly and stealthily through your native forests. This category includes the wild elves (grugach) of Greyhawk and the Kagonesti of Dragonlance, as well as the races called wood elves in Greyhawk and the Forgotten Realms. In Faerun, wood elves (also called wild elves. green elves, or forest elves) are reclusive and distrusting of non-elves.

Wood elves' skin tends to be copperish in hue, sometimes with traces of green. Their hair tends toward browns and blacks, but it is occasionally blond or copper-colored. Their eyes are green, brown, or hazel.

definingFeature.name: Mask of the Wild
definingFeature.desc: Extra speed and the ability to hide even when lightly obscured by nature.
